text: The philosophical soul senses that beneath the reality we inhabit, another, entirely different one lies concealed.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Underneath this reality in which we live and have our being, another and altogether different reality lies concealed...

text: Stupidity has a knack of getting its way.
author: Albert Camus

text: Oh, how distant then was all this resignation from me!
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: How far I was then from all that resignationism!

text: So long as you have food in your mouth, you have solved all questions for the time being.
author: Franz Kafka

text: All life is a nightwatch of expectancy.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Dreams seem to be spurred on not by reason but by desire, not by the head but by the heart.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source_section: The Dream of a Ridiculous Man (1877)

text: When the throne of God is overturned, the rebel must create the justice, order, and unity he sought in vain, thus justifying God's fall. Then begins the desperate effort to forge the dominion of man, even at the price of crime.
author: Albert Camus

text: The mother of excess is not joy, but joylessness.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: The mother of excess is not joy but joylessness.

text: Nothing can discourage the appetite for divinity in the heart of man.
author: Albert Camus

text: The struggle itself toward the heights is enough to fill a man's heart. One must imagine Sisyphus happy.
author: Albert Camus
source: Original French: La lutte elle-même vers les sommets suffit à remplir un cœur d'homme; il faut imaginer Sisyphe heureux.

text: Many are stubborn about the path they have chosen, few about the goal itself.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Destination and paths . Many people are obstinate about the path once it is taken, few people about the destination.

text: If ignoble cowardice and fear are detestable, then 'zeal without wisdom' is no less corrupting and blasphemous.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Søren Kierkegaard, Upbuilding Discourses in Various Spirits , Hong p. 323

text: We often contradict an opinion for no other reason than that we do not like the tone in which it is expressed.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: When questioned, our first opinion is rarely our own, but merely the conventional view of our station; our true thoughts seldom surface.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Our own opinions . The first opinion that occurs to us when we are suddenly asked about a matter is usually not our own, but only the customary one, appropriate to our caste, position, or parentage; our own opinions seldom swim near the surface.

text: To claim that godliness dwindles with childhood, or that repentance is merely a weakness of old age, would be ungodliness and foolishness.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Upbuilding Discourses in Various Spirits , Hong p. 12

text: Money is coined liberty, and so it is ten times dearer to the man who is deprived of freedom.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: The House of the Dead (1862) ch. 1; as translated by Constance Garnett (1915), p. 16

text: Seek first God 's Kingdom, that is, become like the lilies and the birds, become perfectly silent — then shall the rest be added unto you.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: The Lilies of the Field and the Birds of the Air (1849)

text: There was once a community of scoundrels, that is to say, they were not scoundrels, but ordinary people.
author: Franz Kafka

text: It says nothing against the ripeness of a spirit that it has a few worms .
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Hungary, conquered and in chains, has done more for freedom and justice than any people for twenty years. We must never betray what its heroes died for.
author: Albert Camus

text: Once it's proved you're descended from an ape, accept it. Once they've proved a droplet of your own fat is dearer than a hundred thousand fellow humans, and all virtues are mere ravings, accept that too, for there's nothing to be done.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: When... in the course of all these thousands of years has man ever acted in accordance with his own interests?
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Russia was a slave in Europe but would be a master in Asia.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: As quoted in "Dilemmas of Empire 1850-1918: Power, Territory, Identity" by Dominic Livien in Journal of Contemporary History , Vol. 34, No.2 (April 1999), pp. 180

text: The direction of the world overwhelms me. All continents will spill over onto Old Europe. They are hungry and unafraid to die. We no longer know how to die or kill. Europe believes in nothing. I find it harder and harder to live before a wall.
author: Albert Camus
source: Correspondance: 1932-1960, p.220, Gallimard, 1981. Letter to Jean Granier, 1957

text: Since my earliest childhood a barb of sorrow has lodged in my heart. As long as it stays I am ironic — if it is pulled out I shall die.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Integrity has no need of rules. The absurd man accepts no moral code but the dictated, yet he lives outside God. His innocence is to be feared. 'Everything is permitted,' exclaims Ivan Karamazov, not as an outburst of relief or joy, but a bitter acknowledgment of a fact.
author: Albert Camus

text: Artists go forward by going backward, but it is wrong that they stop with historical themes, thinking only these are suitable for poetic treatment. Why are modern heroes not just as poetic?
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: 'I'm glad to know he's [Paneloux] better than his sermon.'
author: Albert Camus

text: ...there are more things to admire in men than to despise.
author: Albert Camus

text: I only know that I was the cause of their sin and downfall. Like a vile trichina, like a germ of the plague infecting whole kingdoms, so I contaminated all this earth, so happy and sinless before my coming. They learnt to lie, grew fond of lying, and discovered the charm of falsehood.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source_section: The Dream of a Ridiculous Man (1877)

text: Here the ways of men part: if you wish to strive for peace of soul and pleasure, then believe; if you wish to be a devotee of truth, then inquire.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Letter to Elisabeth Nietzsche, Bonn, 1865-06-11 [ specific citation needed ] . Quoted in Walter Kaufmann , The Faith of a Heretic (opening epigram).

text: So many men are deprived of grace. How can one live without grace? One has to try it and do what Christianity never did: be concerned with the damned.
author: Albert Camus

text: I don't want harmony. From love for humanity, I don't want it. I would rather be left with unavenged suffering. Too high a price is asked for harmony; it's beyond our means to pay so much to enter on it. And so I hasten to give back my entrance ticket. It's not God that I don't accept, Alyosha, only I most respectfully return him the ticket.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: Book II, Chapter 4: Rebellion (trans. Constance Garnett)

text: Your worst sin is that you have destroyed and betrayed yourself for nothing.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Was he an animal , that music could move him so? He felt as if the way to the unknown nourishment he longed for were coming to light .
author: Franz Kafka

text: If you are penitent, you love. And if you love you are of God. All things are atoned for, all things are saved by love. If I, a sinner even as you are, am tender with you and have pity on you, how much more will God have pity upon you. Love is such a priceless treasure that you can redeem the whole world by it, and cleanse not only your own sins but the sins of others.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Absolute freedom mocks at justice . Absolute justice denies freedom. To be fruitful, the two ideas must find their limits in each other.
author: Albert Camus
source: "Historical Murder", as translated by Anthony Bower

text: There is always a philosophy for the lack of courage.
author: Albert Camus
source: There is always a philosophy for lack of courage.

text: I now myself live, in every detail, striving for wisdom, while I formerly merely worshipped and idolized the wise.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Letter to Mathilde Mayer, July 16, 1878, cited in Karl Jaspers , Nietzsche (Baltimore: 1997), p. 46

text: Nothing is harder to understand than a symbolic work. A symbol always transcends the one who makes use of it and makes him say in reality more than he is aware of expressing.
author: Albert Camus
source: "Hope and the Absurd in the work of Franz Kafka "

text: The world evades us because it becomes itself again. That stage scenery masked by habit becomes what it is. It withdraws at a distance from us.
author: Albert Camus

text: Forgetting, when God does it in relation to sin, is the opposite of creating: to create is to bring forth from nothing, and to forget is to take back into nothing. The one who loves forgives in this way; he forgives, he forgets, he blots out the sin, and in love, he turns toward the one he forgives, unable to see what lies behind his back.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: What the age needs is not a genius — it has had geniuses enough, but a martyr, who in order to teach men to obey would himself be obedient unto death. What the age needs is awakening.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: It's the great mystery of human life that old grief passes gradually into quiet tender joy.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: All that exists that can be denied deserves to be denied; and being truthful means: to believe in an existence that can in no way be denied and which is itself true and without falsehood.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Ages of Life and Truth. - Young people love the interesting and the peculiar, regardless of its truth or falsehood. More mature minds love that in truth which is interesting and peculiar. Fully ripened minds, finally, love truth even where it appears plain and simple and bores the common person, because they have realized that truth, in its highest spiritual essence, often speaks with the guise of simplicity.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Age and truth. Young people love what is interesting and odd, no matter how true or false it is. More mature minds love what is interesting and odd about truth. Fully mature intellects, finally, love truth, even when it appears plain and simple, boring to the ordinary person; for they have noticed that truth tends to reveal its highest wisdom in the guise of simplicity.

text: Above all, avoid falsehood, every kind of falsehood, especially falseness to yourself. Watch over your own deceitfulness and look into it every hour, every minute.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Not one of these nobly equipped young men has escaped the restless, exhausting, confusing, debilitating crisis of education. He feels that he cannot guide himself, cannot help himself—and then he dives hopelessly into the world of everyday life and daily routine, immersed in the most trivial activity possible.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On the Future of Our Educational Institutions (1872)

text: This fact, that the opposite of sin is by no means virtue, has been overlooked. The latter is partly a pagan view, which is content with a merely human standard, and which for that very reason does not know what sin is, that all sin is before God. No, the opposite of sin is faith.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: What is it in us that is mellowed by civilization? All it does is develop in man a capacity to feel a greater variety of sensations. Through this, man will yet learn how to enjoy bloodshed. Civilization has made man, if not always more bloodthirsty, at least more viciously, more horribly bloodthirsty.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: There exists a reciprocal relationship between man and God: the stronger a man is, the weaker God is; the weaker a man is, the stronger God is in him. For God, the infinitely strongest, there is an obstacle He has lovingly posited Himself, every time a man comes into existence, when He in His love makes to be something directly in apposition to Himself. Marvellous love, even His omnipotence is under the sway of love!
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Søren Kierkegaard, Christian Discourses , 1848 Lowrie 1940, 1961 p. 132

text: The educated classes and nations are swept away by a magnificently contemptible money economy. Never was the world more worldly, never poorer in love and kindness.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: The civilized classes and nations are swept away by the grand rush for contemptible wealth. Never was the world worldlier, never was it emptier of love and goodness. “Schopenhauer as educator,” § 3.4

text: When someone hides something behind a bush and finds it there again, there is not much to praise in such seeking and finding. Yet this is how matters stand regarding seeking and finding "truth" within the realm of reason. Such truth is thoroughly anthropomorphic, containing not a single point "true in itself" or universally valid apart from man. At bottom, what the investigator of such truths is seeking is only the metamorphosis of the world into man.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: Those who need myths are indeed poor. Here the gods serve as beds or resting places as the day races across the sky.
author: Albert Camus

text: Granted I am a babbler, a harmless vexatious babbler, like all of us. But what is to be done if the direct and sole vocation of every intelligent man is babble, that is, the intentional pouring of water through a sieve?
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: As Gregor Samsa awoke one morning from uneasy dreams, he found himself transformed in his bed into a monstrous vermin.
author: Franz Kafka
source: As Gregor Samsa awoke one morning from uneasy dreams he found himself transformed in his bed into an enormous insect . First lines

text: Perhaps we cannot prevent this world from being a world in which children are tortured. But we can reduce the number of tortured children. And if you don't help us, who else in the world can help us do this?
author: Albert Camus
source: Said at the Dominican Monastery of Latour-Maubourg (1948); reported in Resistance, Rebellion and Death (translation by Justin O'Brien, 1961), p. 73

text: Love in action is a harsh and dreadful thing compared with love in dreams. Love in dreams is greedy for immediate action, rapidly performed and in the sight of all. But active love is labour and fortitude, and for some people too, perhaps, a complete science.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: I may not have been sure about what really did interest me, but I was absolutely sure about what didn't.
author: Albert Camus

text: The outrage of an individual way of life: all highly individual rules of living provoke others against those who embrace them, for they feel demeaned, as ordinary beings, by the extraordinary treatment such a person grants themselves.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: The infuriating thing about an individual way of living . People are always angry at anyone who chooses very individual standards for his life; because of the extraordinary treatment which that man grants to himself, they feel degraded, like ordinary beings.

text: Is it really not possible to touch the gaming table without being instantly infected by superstition?
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: To judge our classics so falsely and honor them so insultingly, one must no longer know them at all: this is the general truth. For otherwise, one would know there is only one way to honor them: by continuing to seek in their spirit and with their courage, never growing weary.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: In order to be able thus to misjudge, and thus to grant left-handed veneration to our classics, people must have ceased to know them. This, generally speaking, is precisely what has happened. For, otherwise, one ought to know that there is only one way of honoring them, and that is to continue seeking with the same spirit and with the same courage, and not to weary of the search.

text: With all great deceivers there is a noteworthy occurrence to which they owe their power. In the actual act of deception, they are overcome by belief in themselves. It is this which then speaks so miraculously and compellingly to those who surround them.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: But in the end one needs more courage to live than to kill himself.
author: Albert Camus
source_section: A Happy Death (written 1936-38 (published in 1971 over 11 years after the author's death))

text: Yes, there was an element of abstraction and unreality in misfortune. But when an abstraction starts to kill you, you have to get to work on it.
author: Albert Camus

text: Happiness implied a choice, and within that choice a concerted will, a lucid desire.
author: Albert Camus
source_section: A Happy Death (written 1936-38 (published in 1971 over 11 years after the author's death))

text: It is not as a child that I believe and confess Jesus Christ. My hosanna is born of a furnace of doubt.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: As quoted in Kierkegaard, the Melancholy Dane (1950) by Harold Victor Martin.

text: Query: How to contrive not to waste one's time? Answer: By being fully aware of it all the while.
author: Albert Camus

text: Once upon a time, in some out of the way corner of that universe which is dispersed into numberless twinkling solar systems, there was a star upon which clever beasts invented knowing. That was the most arrogant and mendacious minute of "world history," but nevertheless, it was only a minute. After nature had drawn a few breaths, the star cooled and congealed, and the clever beasts had to die. One might invent such a fable, and yet he still would not have adequately illustrated how miserable, how shadowy and transient, how aimless and arbitrary the human intellect looks within nature. There were eternities during which it did not exist. And when it is all over with the human intellect, nothing will have happened.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Variant translation: In some remote corner of the universe, poured out and glittering in innumerable solar systems, there once was a star on which clever animals invented knowledge. That was the highest and most mendacious minute of "world history" — yet only a minute. After nature had drawn a few breaths the star grew cold, and the clever animals had to die. One might invent such a fable and still not have illustrated sufficiently how wretched, how shadowy and flighty, how aimless and arbitrary, the human intellect appears in nature. There have been eternities when it did not exist; and when it is done for again, nothing will have happened.
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: A living man can be enslaved and reduced to the historic condition of an object. But if he dies in refusing to be enslaved, he reaffirms the existence of another kind of human nature which refuses to be classified as an object.
author: Albert Camus
source: quoted in Existentialism Versus Marxism : Conflicting Views on Humanism (1966, New York, Dell Pub. Co., p.234, available on archive.org ), an essay edited by George Edward Novack; this essay includes a collection of texts (abstracts) of authors such as Sartre and Camus (in translation), preceded by brief introductions; this quote is drawn from its Chapter "Albert Camus" (pp.206-240), in the reproduction, in translation, of the section "Totality and trial" from Part III of "The Rebel" by Albert Camus (due to an editorial error in this book edited by Novack, this quote of Camus is often wrongly associated in English with a preceding section titled "the failing of the prophecy"). The exact original quote in French, in L'homme révolté by Albert Camus, Gallimard, 1951, 131th ed., p.293 is On peut asservir un homme vivant et le réduire à l'état historique de chose. Mais s'il meurt en refusant, il réaffirme une nature humaine qui rejette l'ordre des choses. The original French text shows a possible misinterpretation or overinterpretation -or even a possible political bias ?- in this English translation of the second sentence of this quote, the idiom 'order of things', 'l'ordre des choses', having the exact same meaning in French and English, and meaning just how things are, how life works, in a very general sense. Thus Camus here doesn't precisely speak of 'another kind of human nature', but here in this quote may just (re)affirm the constancy of rebellious jolts in the human nature -that's just what the French words litteraly say- (in spite of the considerable plasticity of the human nature, Camus specifies in nearby paragraphs; human nature may be interpreted here as 'repeated actions of some humans', but not all of them, systematically). This view may be easily confirmed by the analysis of the complex full context of this quote, preferably directly in French, the whole Part III of The Rebel , L'homme révolté , which is a harsh critic by Camus of Russian Marxism such as implemented in the USSR of 1951, which precisely, in his view, tries to change or negate the true human nature.

text: "You're a gentleman," they used to say to him. "You shouldn't have gone murdering people with a hatchet; that's no occupation for a gentleman."
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: If God stops a man on the road, and calls him with a revelation and sends him armed with divine authority among men, they say to him; from whom dost thou come? He answers: from God. But now God cannot help his messenger physically like a king, who gives him soldiers or policemen, or his ring or his signature, which is known to all; in short, God cannot help men by providing them with physical certainty that an Apostle is an Apostle-which would, moreover, be nonsense. Even miracles, if the Apostle has that gift, give no physical certainty; for the miracle is the object of faith.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: A sub-clerk in the post office is the equal of a conqueror if consciousness is common to them. A man's failures imply judgment, not of circumstances, but of himself.
author: Albert Camus

text: For everyone strives to keep his individuality as apart as possible, wishing to secure the greatest possible fullness of life for himself; but all his efforts result not in attaining fullness of life but self-destruction, for instead of self-realisation he ends by arriving at complete solitude. Everywhere in these days, men have ceased to understand that true security is found in social solidarity rather than in isolated individual effort. This terrible individualism must inevitably have an end, and all will suddenly understand how unnaturally they are separated from one another.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Oh, can I really believe the poet's tales, that when one first sees the object of one's love, one imagines one has seen her long ago, that all love like all knowledge is remembrance, that love too has its prophecies in the individual. And at the next moment you are so near to me, filling my spirit so powerfully that I am transfigured for myself, and feel that it's good to be here.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Lack of originality, everywhere, all over the world, from time immemorial, has always been considered the foremost quality and the recommendation of the active, efficient and practical man...
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Every earthly good is in itself selfish, begrudging; its possession is envy and must make others poorer – what I have someone else cannot have; the more I have, the less someone else must have. The unrighteous mammon is in itself unjust and makes for injustice, for if one person has much, another must necessarily have little.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Soren Kierkegaard, Christian Discourses 1848, Hong 1997 p. 115-116

text: If God did not exist, everything would be permitted. Everything is indeed permitted, if God does not exist, and man is in consequence forlorn, for he cannot find anything to depend upon either within or outside himself. Man is free, man is freedom.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: Jean-Paul Sartre Existentialism and Humanism , 1948 p. 33-34

text: A genius and an Apostle are qualitatively different, they are definitions which each belong in their own spheres: the sphere of immanence, and the sphere of transcendence.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Outside of that single fatality of death, everything, joy or happiness, is liberty.
author: Albert Camus

text: By believing passionately in something which still does not exist, we create it. The nonexistent is whatever we have not sufficiently desired.
author: Franz Kafka
source: Attributed to Kafka in Ambiguous Spaces (2008) by NaJa & deOstos (Nannette Jackowski and Ricardo de Ostos), p. 7, and a couple other publications since, this is actually from Report to Greco (1965) by Nikos Kazantzakis , p. 434

text: People only count their misfortunes; their good luck they take no account of. But if they were to take everything into account, as they should, they'd find that they had their fair share of it.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Father in heaven, when the thought of thee awakens in our soul, let it not waken as an agitated bird which flutters confusedly about, but as a child waking from sleep with a celestial smile.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Tolerance will reach such a level that intelligent people will be banned from thinking so as not to offend the imbeciles.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: Though attributed to Dostoevsky on social media, there is no record of him making such a statement.

text: Between God and man there is and remains an eternal, essential, qualitative difference.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: We get into the habit of living before acquiring the habit of thinking.
author: Albert Camus

text: To be in love is not the same as loving. You can be in love with a woman and still hate her.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: In civilized society, each person feels superior to others in at least one respect. Upon this rests general goodwill, for everyone is capable of offering help, and thus may accept it without shame.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Everyone superior in one thing . In civilized circumstances, everyone feels superior to everyone else in at least one way ; this is the basis of the general goodwill, inasmuch as everyone is someone who, under certain conditions, can be of help, and need therefore feel no shame in allowing himself to be helped.

text: When sagacity perceives benefit, faith cannot see God; but in the dark night of suffering, when sagacity sees no handbreadth ahead, faith can see God, for faith sees best in the dark.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Uplifting Discourses in Various Spirits in a section of text entitled “The Gospel of Suffering.” As quoted in: Gordon Marino (February 10, 2021): “Faith sees best in the dark”: Joe Biden, Søren Kierkegaard, and the education of suffering. In: ABC Religon & Ethics. Archived from https://www.abc.net.au/religion/biden-kierkegaard-suffering-and-faith-in-the-dark/13139112 on April 16, 2016.

text: Every man knows he is unique and will be in the world only once, yet he hides this truth like a bad conscience, fearing his neighbor's demand for conventionality. Most often, this is due to indolence, for men are lazier than they are timid, fearing the inconveniences of unconditional honesty. Artists alone reveal everyone's secret bad conscience: the law that every man is a unique miracle.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: “ Schopenhauer as educator” ("Schopenhauer als Erzieher"), § 3.1, R. Hollingdale , trans. (1983), p. 127

text: Let this instruct the earnest: I am convinced that art is life's highest task, its truly metaphysical endeavor, in the spirit of the man to whom I dedicate this work as my sublime champion on this path.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Art is the supreme task and the truly metaphysical activity in this life...

text: Slowly, in place of profound interpretation of eternal problems, a historical, even philological, weighing and questioning has emerged. Our students are now encouraged to engage with philosophy in such a neutral manner, leading me to view it as a branch of philology, its practitioners judged by their philological skill. Thus, philosophy itself has been banished from the university.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Philological considerations have slowly but surely taken the place of profound explorations of eternal problems. The question becomes: What did this or that philosopher think or not think? And is this or that text rightly ascribed to him or not? And even: Is this variant of a classical text preferable to that other? Students in university seminars today are encouraged to occupy themselves with such emasculated inquiries. As a result, of course, philosophy itself is banished from the university altogether.
source_section: On the Future of Our Educational Institutions (1872)

text: The world is rejuvenated, but as Heine so wittily remarked, it was rejuvenated by romanticism to such a degree that it became a baby again.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source_section: On the Concept of Irony with Continual Reference to Socrates (1841)

text: How many meek and humble monks there are, yearning for solitude and fervent prayer in peace! From these, perhaps, the salvation of Russia will come once more. For in their solitude, they keep the image of Christ fair and undefiled, in the purity of God's truth, ready to show it to the tottering creeds of the world when the time comes. That star will rise out of the East.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: Book VI, Chapter 3: Conversations and Exhortations of Father Zossima; The Russian Monk and his possible Significance (trans. Constance Garnett)

text: There's no quiet place here on earth for our love, not in the village and not anywhere else, so I picture a grave, deep and narrow, in which we embrace as if clamped together, I bury my face against you, you yours against me, and no one will ever see us.
author: Franz Kafka

text: A man is more a man through the things he keeps to himself than through those he says.
author: Albert Camus

text: The meaning lies in appropriation. Admiration is often envy, criticism often opposition, and recognition in a mirror a fleeting acquaintance—all misunderstandings. But to see correctly and not forget what the mirror cannot effect, that is appropriation, and the appropriation is the reader’s triumphant giving of himself.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Perhaps the two educational maxims are not opposites at all: one says man should have a center, the other that he should also have a periphery. The educating philosopher of whom I dreamed would not only discover the central force, but also know how to prevent its destructive action on other forces: his task would be to mold the whole man into a living solar and planetary system and to understand its higher laws of motion.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: “ Schopenhauer as educator,” § 3.2, R. Hollingdale , trans. (1983), pp. 130-131

text: It’s strange that my work has been classified as magic realism because I see my novels as just being realistic literature. They say that if Kafka had been born in Mexico he would have been a realistic writer. So much depends on where you were born.
author: Franz Kafka

text: They're talking about things of which they don't have the slightest understanding, anyway. It's only because of their stupidity that they're able to be so sure of themselves.
author: Franz Kafka

text: 'But,' I [Dmitri Karamazov] asked, 'how will man be after that? Without God and the future life? It means everything is permitted now, one can do anything?' 'Didn't you know?' he said. And he laughed. 'Everything is permitted to the intelligent man,' he said.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: Book XI, Chapter 4 (trans. Pevear and Volokhonsky)

text: I have needed God every day to defend myself against the abundance of thoughts.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source_section: The Point of View of My Work as an Author (1848)

text: From experience: The unreasonableness of a thing is no argument against its existence, but rather a condition of it.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: From experience . That something is irrational is no argument against its existence, but rather a condition for it.

text: The man guided by concepts only wards off misfortune, gaining no happiness from abstractions. The intuitive man, however, reaps from his intuition a harvest of illumination, cheer, and redemption. Yet, he suffers more intensely and frequently, for he does not learn from experience and keeps falling into the same ditch.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: Love a man even in his sin, for that is the semblance of Divine Love and the highest love on earth. Love all God's creation, the whole of it and every grain of sand. If you love everything, you will perceive the divine mystery in things, and come at last to love the world with an all-embracing love. Everything, like the ocean, flows and enters into contact with everything else: touch one place, and you set up a movement at the other end of the world. Treasure this ecstasy, however absurd people may think it.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: Book VI, Chapter 3: Conversations and Exhortations of Father Zossima; Of Prayer, of Love, and of Contact with other Worlds (trans. Constance Garnett)

text: For decisive religious categories to become the demand of the times is a contradiction in itself. 'The times' is too abstract a category to demand what belongs specifically to individuality and particularity; loud collective demands cannot be made for what can only be shared by the single individual in solitariness and silence.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Two Ages: The Age of Revolution and the Present Age. A Literary Review . By Soren Kierkegaard, 1846 edited and translated by Howard V. Hong and Edna H. Hong 1978 Princeton University Press P. 21

text: So long as man remains free he strives for nothing so incessantly and so painfully as to find some one to worship.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: I do not want to found anything on the incomprehensible. I want to know whether I can live with what I know and with that alone.
author: Albert Camus

text: One does not decide the truth of a thought according to whether it is right-wing or left-wing.
author: Albert Camus
source: Letter to Jean-Paul Sartre , 30 June 1952. As quoted in Paris after the Liberation: 1944-1949 by Antony Beevor and Artemis Cooper .

text: Take a book, the poorest one written, but read it with the passion that it is the only book you will read-ultimately you will read everything out of it, that is, as much as there was in yourself, and you could never get more out of reading, even if you read the best of books.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: One could construe the life of man as a great discourse in which various people represent different parts of speech. How many are mere adjectives, interjections, conjunctions, adverbs? How few are substantives, active verbs, how many are copulas? Human relations are like the irregular verbs in languages where nearly all verbs are irregular.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Socrates marks a singular turning point in world history. For if the immense human energy spent on striving were not in the service of knowledge, but solely for egoistic ends, the instinctive joy of life would so diminish that, through perpetual self-destruction, a gruesome ethics of genocide born of pity might emerge. This plague is averted only where art, in forms like religion and science, appears as a remedy.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: We cannot help but see Socrates as the turning-point, the vortex of world history. For if we imagine that the whole incalculable store of energy used in that global tendency had been used not in the service of knowledge but in ways applied to the practical — selfish — goals of individuals and nations, universal wars of destruction and constant migrations of peoples would have enfeebled man's instinctive zest for life to the point where, suicide having become universal, the individual would perhaps feel a vestigial duty as a son to strangle his parents, or as a friend his friend, as the Fiji islanders do: a practical pessimism that could even produce a terrible ethic of genocide through pity , and which is, and always has been, present everywhere in the world where art has not in some form, particularly as religion and science, appeared as a remedy and means of prevention for this breath of pestilence.

text: Every rebellion implies some kind of unity .
author: Albert Camus

text: For every higher world, one must be born; more precisely, one must be bred for it. A right to philosophy, in the grand sense, is granted only by one's lineage; ancestors, 'blood,' decide even here. Many generations must have labored for the philosopher's emergence; each of his virtues must have been individually acquired, cultivated, inherited, and embodied.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: One sticks one's finger into the soil to tell by the smell in what land one is: I stick my finger in existence — it smells of nothing. Where am I? Who am I? How came I here? What is this thing called the world? What does this world mean? Who is it that has lured me into the world? Why was I not consulted, why not made acquainted with its manners and customs instead of throwing me into the ranks, as if I had been bought by a kidnapper, a dealer in souls? How did I obtain an interest in this big enterprise they call reality? Why should I have an interest in it? Is it not a voluntary concern? And if I am to be compelled to take part in it, where is the director? I should like to make a remark to him. Is there no director? Whither shall I turn with my complaint?
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Job endured everything — until his friends came to comfort him, then he grew impatient.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: In vain do great minds mint new concepts; they merely add to the confusion, for the age's fixed idea is to surpass one's superior.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: A profound thought is in a constant state of becoming; it adopts the experience of a life and assumes its shape. A man's creation is strengthened in its successive aspects, his works complementing, correcting, or even contradicting one another. If creation ends, it is not with the artist's illusory cry, 'I have said everything,' but with the death of the creator, which closes his experiences and the book of his genius. There is no true creation without a secret.
author: Albert Camus

text: Like a dog!" he said, it was as if the shame of it should outlive him.
author: Franz Kafka

text: Knowing whether or not one can live without appeal is all that interests me.
author: Albert Camus

text: Ours is a lost generation, it may be, but it is more blameless than those earlier generations.
author: Franz Kafka

text: But if it were possible, through a strong will, to overturn the entire past of the world, we would immediately join the ranks of independent gods, and world history would then be for us nothing but a dreamlike self-rapture; the curtain falls, and man finds himself again, like a child playing with worlds, like a child who awakens at dawn and laughingly wipes the terrible dreams from his brow.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: As soon as it becomes possible, by dint of a strong will, to overthrow the entire past of the world, then, in a single moment, we will join the ranks of independent gods. World history for us will then be nothing but a dreamlike otherworldly being. The curtain falls, and man once more finds himself a child playing with whole worlds—a child, awoken by the first glow of morning, who laughingly wipes the frightful dreams from his brow. "Fatum und Geschichte," April 1862

text: To find the universal, what is common to all human beings. Oh, how beautiful to lose oneself in this way. Yet, to be concerned for oneself, as if the particular individual were all there was. Oh, how beautiful thus to find oneself in the universal!
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: S. Kierkegaard 1846 Journals , Hannay 1996, VII IB200, p. 252

text: What terrible tragedies realism inflicts on people.'
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: If there is a sin against life, it consists perhaps not so much in despairing of life as in hoping for another life and in eluding the implacable grandeur of this life.
author: Albert Camus
source: "Summer in Algiers" , The Myth of Sisyphus and Other Essays (1955)

text: The whole visible world is perhaps nothing more than the rationalization of a man who wants to find peace for a moment.
author: Franz Kafka

text: It is the duty of the human understanding to understand that there are things which it cannot understand, and what those things are.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: ...hope: in truth, it is the worst of evils, because it prolongs the torments of man.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: In reality, hope is the worst of all evils, because it prolongs man's torments.

text: I dream of a grave, deep and narrow, where we could clasp each other in our arms as with clamps, and I would hide my face in you and you would hide my face in me, and nobody would ever see us any more
author: Franz Kafka

text: If the genius is an artist, then he accomplishes his work as art, but neither he nor his work of art has a telos outside him.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: To be truthful means to employ the usual metaphors. It is a duty to lie according to a fixed convention, to lie with the herd. Through this unconsciousness and forgetfulness, man arrives at his sense of truth.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: All systems of morality are based on the idea that an action has consequences that legitimize or cancel it. A mind imbued with the absurd merely judges that those consequences must be considered calmly. There may be responsible persons, but there are no guilty ones.
author: Albert Camus

text: Deep within every human being there still lives the anxiety over the possibility of being alone in the world, forgotten by God, overlooked among millions. One keeps this anxiety at a distance by looking at kin and friends, but it is still there, and one hardly dares think of how he would feel if all this were taken away.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: To create is likewise to give a shape to one's fate. There is no frontier between being and appearing.
author: Albert Camus

text: To two men living the same number of years, the world always provides the same sum of experiences. It is up to us to be conscious of them.
author: Albert Camus

text: Only through rigorous self-discipline in one's mother tongue can one acquire a physical loathing for the 'elegant' and 'ornate' styles of superficial writers. Culture begins with the correct movement of language, fostering a profound disgust for mere elegance.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On the Future of Our Educational Institutions (1872)

text: Plenty of hope — for God — no end of hope — only not for us.
author: Franz Kafka
source: In conversation with Max Brod (1920), after Brod had queried on there being "hope outside this manifestation of the world that we know", as quoted in Franz Kafka: A Biography [ Franz Kafka, eine Biographie ] (1937) by Max Brod, as translated by G. Humphreys Roberts and Richard Winston (1947; 1960); at least as early as Franz Kafka : Parable and Paradox (1962) by Heinz Politzer, this assertion has often appeared paraphrased as: " There is hope, but not for us ", and sometimes "There is hope — only not for us."

text: Idleness is the beginning of all vice, the crown of all virtues.
author: Franz Kafka

text: When remorse calls, it is always late, always the eleventh hour. Whether young or old, whether much sinned or little, guilt makes this call come at the eleventh hour. Repentance and remorse belong to the eternal in a man.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Søren Kierkegaard, Purity of Heart is to Will One Thing , 1847 p. 40-41

text: The formula 'two plus two equals five' is not without its attractions.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: This heart within me I can feel , and I judge that it exists . This world I can touch , and I likewise judge that it exists. There ends all my knowledge , and the rest is construction. For if I try to seize this self of which I feel sure , if I try to define and to summarize it, it is nothing but water slipping through my fingers. I can sketch one by one all the aspects it is able to assume, all those likewise that have been attributed to it, this upbringing, this origin, this ardor or these silences , this nobility or this vileness. But aspects cannot be added up. This very heart which is mine will forever remain indefinable to me. Between the certainty I have of my existence and the content I try to give to that assurance, the gap will never be filled. Forever I shall be a stranger to myself.
author: Albert Camus

text: The preceding merely defines a way of thinking. But the point is to live.
author: Albert Camus

text: Not merely in commerce but in the world of ideas, our age organizes a clearance sale. Everything is to be had at such a bargain that it is questionable whether anyone will want to bid.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: To work and create "for nothing," to sculpture in clay , to know one's creation has no future, to see one's work destroyed in a day while being aware that fundamentally this has no more importance than building for centuries — this is the difficult wisdom that absurd thought sanctions. Performing these two tasks simultaneously, negating on the one hand and magnifying on the other, it the way open to the absurd creator. He must give the void its colors.
author: Albert Camus

text: A man once said: Why such reluctance? If you only followed the parables you yourselves would become parables and with that rid of all your daily cares. Another said: I bet that is also a parable. The first said: You have won. The second said: But unfortunately only in parable. The first said: No, in reality: in parable you have lost.
author: Franz Kafka
source: "On Parables" (1922), translation by Willa and Edwin Muir

text: Are designations congruent with things? Is language the adequate expression of all realities? It is only by means of forgetfulness that man can ever reach the point of fancying himself to possess a "truth" of the grade just indicated. If he will not be satisfied with truth in the form of tautology, that is to say, if he will not be content with empty husks, then he will always exchange truths for illusions.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: After all, in the poets love has its priests, and sometimes one hears a voice which knows how to defend it; but of faith one hears never a word. Who speaks in honor of this passion? Philosophy goes further. Theology sits rouged at the window and courts its favor, offering to sell her charms to philosophy. it is supposed to be difficult to understand Hegel, but to understand Abraham is a trifle. To go beyond Hegel's is a miracle, but to get beyond Abraham is the easiest thing of all.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: They showed me their trees , and I could not understand the intense love with which they looked at them; it was as though they were talking with creatures like themselves. And perhaps I shall not be mistaken if I say that they conversed with them. Yes, they had found their language, and I am convinced that the trees understood them. They looked at all Nature like that — at the animals who lived in peace with them and did not attack them, but loved them, conquered by their love. They pointed to the stars and told me something about them which I could not understand, but I am convinced that they were somehow in touch with the stars, not only in thought, but by some living channel.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source_section: The Dream of a Ridiculous Man (1877)

text: The papers were always talking about the debt owed to society . According to them, it had to be paid. But that doesn't speak to the imagination . What really counted was the possibility of escape , a leap to freedom , out of the implacable ritual, a wild run for it that would give whatever chance for hope there was. Of course, hope meant being cut down on some street corner, as you ran like mad, by a random bullet. But when I really thought it through, nothing was going to allow me such a luxury. Everything was against it; I would just be caught up in the machinery again.
author: Albert Camus

text: Fools admire everything in an author of reputation.
author: Voltaire

text: Poor and free, rather than rich and enslaved. Of course, men desire to be both rich and free, and it is this very desire that sometimes leads them to be poor and enslaved.
author: Albert Camus
source: Poor and free rather than rich and enslaved. Of course, men want to be both rich and free, and this is what leads them at times to be poor and enslaved.

text: The children of the sun , the children of their sun — oh, how beautiful they were! Never had I seen on our own earth such beauty in mankind. Only perhaps in our children, in their earliest years, one might find, some remote faint reflection of this beauty. The eyes of these happy people shone with a clear brightness. Their faces were radiant with the light of reason and fullness of a serenity that comes of perfect understanding, but those faces were gay; in their words and voices there was a note of childlike joy. Oh, from the first moment, from the first glance at them, I understood it all! It was the earth untarnished by the Fall; on it lived people who had not sinned. They lived just in such a paradise as that in which, according to all the legends of mankind, our first parents lived before they sinned; the only difference was that all this earth was the same paradise. These people, laughing joyfully, thronged round me and caressed me; they took me home with them, and each of them tried to reassure me. Oh, they asked me no questions, but they seemed, I fancied, to know everything without asking, and they wanted to make haste to smoothe away the signs of suffering from my face.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source_section: The Dream of a Ridiculous Man (1877)

text: With Ariadne's thread, the labyrinth's mazes and its monster are easily overcome. But how many plunge into life's labyrinth without such a guide?
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Happiness demands time. A great deal of time. For happiness, too, is a long patience.
author: Albert Camus
source: Only it takes time to be happy. A lot of time. Happiness , too, is a long patience .
source_section: A Happy Death (written 1936-38 (published in 1971 over 11 years after the author's death))

text: The question is asked in ignorance, by one who does not even know what can have led him to ask it.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: As a flood spreads wider and wider, the water becomes shallower and dirtier. The Revolution evaporates, and leaves behind only the slime of a new bureaucracy. The chains of tormented mankind are made out of red tape.
author: Franz Kafka
source: Gustav Janouch, Conversations With Kafka: Notes and Reminiscences (1953), page 71, London: Derek Verschoyle; frequently quoted as " Every revolution evaporates, and leaves behind only the slime of a new bureaucracy."

text: What is truth but to live for an idea? When all is said and done, everything is based on a postulate; but not until one lives in it, does it cease to be a postulate.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: We are accountable to ourselves for our existence; therefore, let us be its true helmsmen, and not allow our lives to be governed by thoughtless chance.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: We are responsible to ourselves for our own existence; consequently we want to be the true helmsman of this existence and refuse to allow our existence to resemble a mindless act of chance. “ Schopenhauer as educator,” § 3.1, R. Hollingdale , trans. (1983), p. 128

text: This was her finest role and the hardest one to play. Choosing between heaven and a ridiculous fidelity, preferring oneself to eternity or losing oneself in God is the age-old tragedy in which each must play his part.
author: Albert Camus

text: There is no more dreadful punishment than futile and hopeless labor.
author: Albert Camus

text: What I really need is to get clear about what I must do, not what I must know, except insofar as knowledge must precede every act. What matters is to find a purpose, to see what it really is that God wills that I shall do; the crucial thing is to find a truth which is truth for me, to find the idea for which I am willing to live and die.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Journal entry, Gilleleie (1 August 1835) Journals 1A; this is considered to be one of the earliest statements of existentialist thought.

text: Mathematics would certainly have not come into existence if one had known from the beginning that there was in nature no exactly straight line, no actual circle, no absolute magnitude.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: As quoted in The Puzzle Instinct : The Meaning of Puzzles in Human Life‎ (2004) by Marcel Danesi, p. 71 from Human All-Too-Human

text: The immediacy of falling in love recognizes but one immediacy that is of equal standing, and this is a religious immediacy; falling in love is too virginal to recognize any confidant other than God.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Man is the only creature who refuses to be what he is.
author: Albert Camus

text: You think your pain and your heartbreak are unprecedented in the history of the world, but then you read. It was Dostoevsky and Dickens who taught me that the things that tormented me most were the very things that connected me with all the people who were alive, or who ever had been alive. Only if we face these open wounds in ourselves can we understand them in other people. An artist is a sort of emotional or spiritual historian. His role is to make you realize the doom and glory of knowing who you are and what you are. He has to tell, because nobody else can tell, what it is like to be alive.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: James Baldwin in "Doom and glory of knowing who you are" by Jane Howard, in LIFE magazine, Vol. 54, No. 21 (24 May 1963)

text: It's easier for a Russian to become an atheist than for anyone else in the world.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: I proclaim that I believe in nothing and that everything is absurd, yet I cannot doubt the validity of my proclamation; I must at least believe in my protest. The first and only evidence supplied by the absurd is rebellion, born of irrationality confronted with an unjust and incomprehensible condition.
author: Albert Camus
source: pp. 8 - 10 as quoted in Albert Camus and the Philosophy of the Absurd ;(2002) by Avi Sagi, p. 44

text: Life as life's own yield. However far man may extend his knowledge, however objective he may appear to himself, ultimately he carries away nothing but his own biography.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Life as the product of life . However far man may extend himself with his knowledge, however objective he may appear to himself - ultimately he reaps nothing but his own biography.

text: Is life not a hundred times too short to be bored?
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Is not life a hundred times too short for us— to bore ourselves?

text: In all their lives, they seemed to do nothing but admire one another; it was like being in love, but an all-embracing, universal feeling.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source_section: The Dream of a Ridiculous Man (1877)

text: Who consciously throws himself into the water or onto the knife?
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Affection of the mild but steady order is the kind that wears best.
author: Albert Camus

text: One who cannot come to terms with life while alive, though struggling with despair, sees different and more among the ruins than others. Dead in his own lifetime, he is the real survivor.
author: Franz Kafka

text: The multitude of languages reveals that words are never a question of truth, nor of adequate expression. Language merely designates the relations of things to men, and for this, it lays hold of the boldest metaphors.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: He discovered the cruel paradox by which we always deceive ourselves twice about the people we love — first to their advantage, then to their disadvantage.
author: Albert Camus
source_section: A Happy Death (written 1936-38 (published in 1971 over 11 years after the author's death))

text: The venerability, reliability, and utility of truth is demonstrated by contrasting it with the liar, whom no one trusts and everyone excludes.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: I yearned for suffering, longed that my blood should be drained to the last drop in these agonies. But they only laughed, then declared me dangerous. Such grief then wrung my heart, and I felt as though dying; and then... I awoke.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source_section: The Dream of a Ridiculous Man (1877)

text: My life has changed, yet at bottom, it remains unchanged. From the very beginning, I sensed a discrepancy, a maladjustment, causing discomfort that even public functions could not eliminate. Often, the mere look of a loved one would fill me with helpless embarrassment, fear, even despair.
author: Franz Kafka

text: Socialism itself can hope to exist only for brief periods here and there, and then only through the exercise of the extremest terrorism. For this reason it is secretly preparing itself for rule through fear and is driving the word 'justice' into the heads of the half-educated masses like a nail so as to rob them of their reason... and to create in them a good conscience for the evil game they are to play.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: For socialism is not merely the labour question, it is before all things the atheistic question, the question of the form taken by atheism to-day, the question of the tower of Babel built without God, not to mount to heaven from earth but to set up heaven on earth.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: It's better to bet on this life than on the next.
author: Albert Camus
source_section: A Happy Death (written 1936-38 (published in 1971 over 11 years after the author's death))

text: If the world were clear, art would not exist.
author: Albert Camus

text: To pray for one's enemies is the hardest thing of all.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: A Roman emperor surrounded by his bodyguard is a magnificent sight, but when the reason is fear, the magnificence pales.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Two Ages: The Age of Revolution and the Present Age. A Literary Review . By Soren Kierkegaard, 1846 edited and translated by Howard V. Hong and Edna H. Hong 1978 Princeton University Press P. 68

text: That which does not kill us makes us stronger.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Friedrich Nietzsche, Twilight of the Idols (1889)

text: At any street corner the feeling of absurdity can strike any man in the face.
author: Albert Camus

text: The important thing isn't the soundness or otherwise of the argument, but for it to make you think.
author: Albert Camus

text: There always comes a time in history when the person who dares to say that 2+2=4 is punished by death. And the issue is not what reward or what punishment will be the outcome of that reasoning. The issue is simply whether or not 2+2=4.
author: Albert Camus

text: Arrogance on the part of the meritorious is even more offensive to us than the arrogance of those without merit: for merit itself is offensive .
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Let others complain that the age is wicked; my complaint is that it is paltry, for it lacks passion. Men's thoughts are thin and flimsy like lace, their passions sleepy.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: The prerogative of greatness: to bestow profound joy with but a modest gift.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Privilege of greatness . It is the privilege of greatness to grant supreme pleasure through trifling gifts.

text: To care only for well-being seems to me positively ill-bred.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: What is hell? I maintain that it is the suffering of being unable to love.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: The church bells call to prayer, but not in a temple made by human hands. Ought men not be moved to prayer in the true house of God, where heaven's arch forms the ceiling, and the birds' song, the congregational hymn?
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: It belongs to the imperfection of everything human that man can only attain his desire by passing through its opposite.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Pledging love 'throughout eternity' means little, for if one is finished with time, one is probably finished with eternity. Far more meaningful would be a vow 'until Easter, until next May Day,' for then they would speak of something tangible, something they might actually achieve.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Style ought to prove that one believes in an idea; not only that one thinks it but also feels it.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: In most cases, people, even the most vicious, are much more naive and simple-minded than we assume them to be. And this is true of ourselves too.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: The chief thing is to love others as yourself, yet this ancient truth, told a billion times, has not formed part of our lives. The consciousness of life is higher than life, the knowledge of the laws of happiness is higher than happiness—that is what one must contend against.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source_section: The Dream of a Ridiculous Man (1877)

text: Remembering is sad, its object even more so. It only weakens one’s present position without strengthening the past.
author: Franz Kafka

text: Let us suppose that a man believes in eternal life on Christ's word. In that case he believes without any fuss about being profound and searching and philosophical and racking his brains.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Old age realizes the dreams of youth: look at Dean Swift ; in his youth he built an asylum for the insane, in his old age he was himself an inmate.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: The slave begins by demanding justice and ends by wanting to wear a crown. He must dominate in his turn.
author: Albert Camus

text: Man grows used to everything, the scoundrel.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Now the Sirens have a still more fatal weapon than their song, namely their silence... Someone might possibly have escaped from their singing; but from their silence, certainly never.
author: Franz Kafka

text: To stand on one leg and prove God's existence is a very different thing from going on one's knees and thanking Him.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: So pervasive are deception, flattery, and the continuous fluttering around the solitary flame of vanity among men, that it is almost incomprehensible how an honest and pure drive for truth could have arisen among them.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Variant translation: The constant fluttering around the single flame of vanity is so much the rule and the law that almost nothing is more incomprehensible than how an honest and pure urge for truth could make its appearance among men.
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: If the lazy are said to kill time, then a period that stakes its salvation on public opinion—that is, on private laziness—must truly fear its own demise: that it will be struck from the history of life's true liberation. How great must be the revulsion of later generations for the legacy of an age ruled not by living beings, but by publicly opinionated phantoms.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: If it is true to say of the lazy that they kill time, then it is greatly to be feared that an era which sees its salvation in public opinion, this is to say private laziness, is a time that really will be killed: I mean that it will be struck out of the history of the true liberation of life. How reluctant later generations will be to have anything to do with the relics of an era ruled, not by living men, but by pseudo-men dominated by public opinion. “ Schopenhauer as educator,” § 3.1, R. Hollingdale , trans. (1983), p. 128

text: On the mountain of truth, you never climb in vain: either you ascend higher today, or you strengthen yourself to climb higher tomorrow.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: In the mountains of truth you will never climb in vain : either you will get up higher today or you will exercise your strength so as to be able to get up higher tomorrow. II.293, maxim 358

text: Even that has its reason; it is often better to be in chains than to be free.
author: Franz Kafka

text: It would have been so pointless to kill himself that, even if he had wanted to, the pointlessness would have made him unable.
author: Franz Kafka

text: To possess wealth is to be freed from its dominion.
author: Albert Camus
source: Having money is a way of being free of money.
source_section: A Happy Death (written 1936-38 (published in 1971 over 11 years after the author's death))

text: I have just now come from a party where I was its life and soul; witticisms streamed from my lips, everyone laughed and admired me, but I went away and wanted to shoot myself.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: They had no temples, but they had a real living and uninterrupted sense of oneness with the whole of the universe; they had no creed, but they had a certain knowledge that when their earthly joy had reached the limits of earthly nature, then there would come for them, for the living and for the dead, a still greater fullness of contact with the whole of the universe. They looked forward to that moment with joy, but without haste, not pining for it, but seeming to have a foretaste of it in their hearts, of which they talked to one another.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source_section: The Dream of a Ridiculous Man (1877)

text: He who humbleth himself wants to be exalted.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: There is no sin, and there can be no sin on all the earth, which the Lord will not forgive to the truly repentant! Man cannot commit a sin so great as to exhaust the infinite love of God.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Does it matter whether it was a dream or reality, if the dream made known to me the truth? If once one has recognized the truth and seen it, you know that it is the truth and that there is no other and there cannot be, whether you are asleep or awake. That real life of which you make so much I had meant to extinguish by suicide, and my dream — oh, it revealed to me a different life, renewed, grand and full of power!
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source_section: The Dream of a Ridiculous Man (1877)

text: The worst readers are those who behave like plundering troops: they take away a few things they can use, dirty and confound the remainder, and revile the whole.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: There lay certitude; there, in the daily round. All the rest hung on mere threads and trivial contingencies; you couldn't waste your time on it. The thing was to do your job as it should be done.
author: Albert Camus

text: No one talks more passionately about his rights than he who in the depths of his soul doubts whether he has any. By enlisting passion on his side he wants to stifle his reason and its doubts: thus he will acquire a good conscience and with it success among his fellow men.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: The words that reverberate for us at the confines of this long adventure of rebellion are not formulas for optimism, for which we have no possible use in the extremities of our unhappiness, but words of courage and intelligence which, on the shores of the eternal seas, even have the qualities of virtue.
author: Albert Camus

text: I can prove at any time that my education tried to make another person out of me than the one I became. It is for the harm, therefore, that my educators could have done me in accordance with their intentions that I reproach them; I demand from their hands the person I now am, and since they cannot give him to me, I make of my reproach and laughter a drumbeat sounding in the world beyond.
author: Franz Kafka

text: There are ages in which the rational man and the intuitive man stand side by side, the one in fear of intuition, the other with scorn for abstraction. The latter is just as irrational as the former is inartistic. They both desire to rule over life: the former, by knowing how to meet his principle needs by means of foresight, prudence, and regularity; the latter, by disregarding these needs and, as an 'overjoyed hero,' counting as real only that life which has been disguised as illusion and beauty.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: 'At my age one's got to be sincere. Lying's too much effort.'
author: Albert Camus

text: Every false idea culminates in bloodshed, yet it is always the blood of others. This is why some of our philosophers feel so comfortable uttering any absurdity.
author: Albert Camus
source: Mistaken ideas always end in bloodshed, but in every case it is someone else's blood. That is why some of our thinkers feel free to say just about anything. Actuelles I, 1950

text: '...it is not necessary to accept everything as true, one must only accept it as necessary.' 'A melancholy conclusion,' said K. 'It turns lying into a universal principle.'
author: Franz Kafka

text: The whole art of Kafka consists in forcing the reader to re-read. His endings or his absence of endings, suggest explanations which, however, are not revealed in clear language but, before they seem justified, require that the story be reread.
author: Franz Kafka
source: Albert Camus , Hope and The Absurd in the Work of Franz Kafka.

text: Life can be magnificent and overwhelming — that is its whole tragedy. Without beauty, love, or danger, it would be almost easy to live. The realization that life is absurd cannot be an end, but only a beginning. It is not this discovery that is interesting, but the consequences and rules of action drawn from it.
author: Albert Camus
source: Review of Nausea by Jean-Paul Sartre , published in the newspaper Alger Républicain (20 October 1938), p. 5; also quoted in Albert Camus and the Philosophy of the Absurd (2002) by Avi Sagi, p. 43

text: A witticism is an epigram on the death of a feeling.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: A Path to Equality. - A few hours of mountain climbing turn a rascal and a saint into two pretty similar creatures. Fatigue is the shortest way to Equality and Fraternity--and, in the end, Liberty will surrender to Sleep.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Woe betide fateful curiosity should it ever peer through the chamber of consciousness and glimpse that humanity, in its ignorant indifference, rests on the pitiless, the greedy, the insatiable, the murderous—clinging, as it were, to the back of a tiger.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: The drive toward the formation of metaphors is the fundamental human drive; to dispense with it is to dispense with man himself. This drive is scarcely subdued by the rigid world of concepts built as its prison. It seeks new realms in myth and art, continually desiring to refashion the world of waking man to be as colorful and eternally new as the world of dreams. When the rigid web of concepts is torn by art, man sometimes thinks he must be dreaming.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: If you have hitherto believed that life was one of the highest value and now see yourselves disappointed, do you at once have to reduce it to the lowest possible price?
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: I will make an attempt to attain freedom, the youthful soul says to itself; and is it to be hindered by the fact that two nations hate and fight, or two continents are separated by an ocean, or a religion is taught that did not exist a couple of thousand years ago? All that is not you, it says to itself.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: “ Schopenhauer as educator,” § 3.1, R. Hollingdale , trans. (1983), p. 128

text: Many a man fails to become a thinker only because his memory is too good.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Many a man fails to become a thinker only because his memory is too good.

text: Our technical civilization has just reached its greatest level of savagery. We will have to choose, in the more or less near future, between collective suicide and the intelligent use of our scientific conquests. What will it bring to a world already given over to all the convulsions of violence, incapable of any control, indifferent to justice and the simple happiness of men—a world where science devotes itself to organized murder?
author: Albert Camus

text: The Messiah will come only when he is no longer necessary; he will come only on the day after his arrival; he will come, not on the last day, but on the very last day.
author: Franz Kafka
source: Variant translation: The Messiah will come only when he is no longer necessary; he will come only on the day after his arrival; he will come, not on the last day, but at the very last.

text: Blessed are the hearts that can bend; they shall never be broken.
author: Albert Camus
source_section: A Happy Death (written 1936-38 (published in 1971 over 11 years after the author's death))

text: God creates out of nothing. Wonderful you say. Yes, to be sure, but He does what is still more wonderful: He makes saints out of sinners.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Sin is man's destruction. Only the rust of sin can consume the soul or eternally destroy it. Unlike bodily sickness, sin is not a passage to be endured, but an eternal fall from the eternal. There is an infinite distinction between suffering and sin, for sin, and sin only, is truly destructive.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Søren Kierkegaard, Christian Discourses, The Joy of it – That We Suffer Only Once But Triumph Eternally. P. 108 Lowrie Translation 1961 Oxford University Press

text: In the solemn silence before God, where no one else is present, the rule holds: either hold to Him or despise Him. There is no excuse, for in that silence, God's closeness is undeniable.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source_section: The Lily of the Field and the Bird of the Air (May 14, 1849)

text: If a man, with all his reasoning, sought to explain love to a girl deeply in love, and she admitted his explanation was correct, would she not choose his miserable common sense over the wealth of her passion?
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: The essence of humanity, distinguishing us from beasts, is our power to transmute vivid perceptions into abstract concepts. From this, we forge a new world of laws and order, a reality more solid, universal, and human than the immediate world of sense.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: No one can build the bridge upon which you must cross the river of life, no one but you alone.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: No one can construct for you the bridge upon which precisely you must cross the stream of life, no one but you yourself alone. “ Schopenhauer as educator,” § 3.1, R. Hollingdale , trans. (1983), p. 129

text: The objective of all human arrangements is through distracting one's thoughts to cease to be aware of life.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Death prompts the sensual to cry: 'Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die!' Yet this is but sensuality's cowardly lust for life, a contemptible order where one lives to eat and drink, rather than eating and drinking to live.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: There is no fate that cannot be surmounted by scorn. If the descent is sometimes performed in sorrow, it can also take place in joy.
author: Albert Camus

text: Forgetting our intentions is the most frequent of all acts of stupidity.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: A novel is philosophy put into images. When philosophy overflows the characters, the novel loses its life. The secret fusion of experiences and ideas, of life and reflection on its meaning, is what makes the great novelist.
author: Albert Camus
source: Review of Nausea by Jean-Paul Sartre , published in the newspaper Alger Républicain (20 October 1938), p. 5; reprinted in Selected Essays and Notebooks , translated and edited by Philip Thody

text: Autumn is a second Spring when every leaf is a flower .
author: Albert Camus
source: As quoted in Visions from Earth (2004) by James R. Miller, p. 126

text: In Oran, as elsewhere, for want of time and thought, people have to love one another without knowing it.
author: Albert Camus

text: How do we rediscover ourselves? How can man truly know himself? He is a dark and veiled enigma. If a hare has seven skins, man can shed himself seventy times seven and still not declare: 'This is truly you; this is no longer a mere shell.'
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: How can a man know himself? He is a thing dark and veiled; and if the hare has seven skins, man can slough off seventy times seven and still not be able to say: “this is really you, this is no longer outer shell.” “ Schopenhauer as educator,” § 3.1, R. Hollingdale , trans. (1983), p. 129

text: Can one be a saint without God?, that's the problem, in fact the only problem, I'm up against today.
author: Albert Camus

text: Man stands face to face with the irrational, feeling within him his longing for happiness and reason. The absurd is born of this confrontation between the human need and the unreasonable silence of the world.
author: Albert Camus

text: The greatest saving one can make in the order of thought is to accept the unintelligibility of the world — and to pay attention to man.
author: Albert Camus

text: Every tradition grows ever more venerable — the more remote its origin, the more confused that origin is. The reverence due to it increases from generation to generation. The tradition finally becomes holy and inspires awe.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: In the heart of nature, free from life's nauseating air, the soul breathes freely and opens to every noble impression. Here, one feels both nature's master and bows before a higher power, surrendering to the force that rules all, feeling great and small at once.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Self-accusation is the possibility of justification.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Three Discourses at Friday Communion November 14, 1849 Hong translation 1997 P. 132
source_section: Three Discourses at the Communion on Fridays (Nov 13, 1849)

text: At the heart of every profound thought, there is something incommunicable, a truth that remains forever within, and with which one dies, perhaps without ever sharing the most important of ideas.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: What men avoid in the liar is not deception itself, but its harmful consequences. Similarly, they desire truth not for its own sake, but for its pleasant, life-preserving effects, remaining indifferent to consequence-free knowledge, and hostile to truths that might harm.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)
