text: There is not love of life without despair about life.
author: Albert Camus

text: Spontaneous love, even in its happiness, loves with the power of despair.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: Human justice is very prolix, and yet at times quite mediocre; divine justice is more concise and needs no information from the prosecution, no legal papers, no interrogation of witnesses, but makes the guilty one his own informer and helps him with eternity's memory.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Eighteen Upbuilding Discourses, Against Cowardliness p. 351

text: Wretched ephemeral race, children of chance and toil, why do you compel me to tell you what it is most salutary for you not to hear? The very best thing for you is utterly beyond your reach: not to be born, not to be, to be nothing. The second best, however, is to die soon.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: According to the old story, King Midas had long hunted wise Silenus , Dionysus' companion, without catching him. When Silenus had finally fallen into his clutches, the king asked him what was the best and most desirable thing of all for mankind. The daemon stood still, stiff and motionless, until at last, forced by the king, he gave a shrill laugh and spoke these words: 'Miserable, ephemeral race, children of hazard and hardship, why do you force me to say what it would be much more fruitful for you not to hear? The best of all things is something entirely outside your grasp: not to be born, not to be , to be nothing . But the second-best thing for you — is to die soon.'

text: Life is, after all, not a product of morality.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Preface 1, tr. R.J. Hollingdale. The German original has slightly other meaning: "das Leben ist nun einmal nicht von der Moral ausgedacht " ("...and the life was not invented, one day, by morality").

text: What must be remembered in any case is that secret complicity that joins the logical and the everyday to the tragic.
author: Albert Camus
source: "Hope and the Absurd in the work of Franz Kafka"

text: Believe me, there is no such thing as great suffering, great regret, great memory...Everything is forgotten, even great love.
author: Albert Camus
source_section: A Happy Death (written 1936-38 (published in 1971 over 11 years after the author's death))

text: The history of mankind is the instant between two strides taken by a traveler.
author: Franz Kafka

text: The world is subjected to the law of imperfection and indifference: he who does not work often gets the bread, and he who sleeps may get it more abundantly than the man who toils. To him who has the world's treasure, it is given, however he got it.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: The immense framework of concepts to which man clings for preservation is but a scaffolding for the liberated intellect. When the intellect smashes this framework, it demonstrates its need for intuition over concepts. For these intuitions, no words exist; man grows dumb, or speaks only in forbidden metaphors and unheard-of combinations, shattering old conceptual barriers to creatively meet the powerful present intuition.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: In the fear-ridden rush into a crowd, it is deemed bold not to fear, not even God. Yet, the individual who truly fears God, not the crowd, becomes a target of ridicule. But let us not dismiss the tradition that a man should truly fear God. This fear is known to the individual conscious of eternal responsibility before God.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Søren Kierkegaard, Purity of Heart , 1847 Steere translation p. 196-197

text: There are many people who reach their conclusions about life like schoolboys; they cheat their master by copying the answer out of a book without having worked out the sum for themselves.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: If a man has his eyes bound, you can encourage him as much as you like to stare through the bandage, but he'll never see anything
author: Franz Kafka

text: I don't know whether this world has a meaning that transcends it. But I know that I cannot know that meaning and that it is impossible for me just now to know it. What can a meaning outside my condition mean to me? I can understand only in human terms. What I touch, what resists me — that I understand. And these two certainties — my appetite for the absolute and for unity and the impossibility of reducing this world to a rational and reasonable principle — I also know that I cannot reconcile them. What other truth can I admit without lying, without bringing in a hope I lack and which means nothing within the limits of my conditions?
author: Albert Camus

text: The stupider one is, the closer one is to reality. The stupider one is, the clearer one is. Stupidity is brief and artless, while intelligence wriggles and hides itself. Intelligence is a knave, but stupidity is honest and straightforward.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: We always deceive ourselves twice about those we love: first, in their favor, then to their detriment.
author: Albert Camus
source: We always deceive ourselves twice about the people we love — first to their advantage, then to their disadvantage. A Happy Death (written 1938), first published as La mort heureuse (1971), as translated by Richard Howard (1972)

text: Where lies the boundary between lack of will and lack of ability, between indolence and the limits of finitude? No dialectician can decide this for a particular individual.
author: Soren Kierkegaard

text: All that I know most surely about morality and the obligations of men, I owe to football.
author: Albert Camus
source: "Ce que je sais de plus sûr à propos de la moralité et des obligations des hommes, c'est au sport que je le dois", sentence parfois modifiée en : "C'est au football que je le dois !"

text: The similarity between Christ and Socrates consists essentially in their dissimilarity. Just as philosophy begins with doubt, so also a life that may be called human begins with irony.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source_section: On the Concept of Irony with Continual Reference to Socrates (1841)

text: There is a cowardice that wants certainty before it begins, and an upside-downness that wants to reap before it sows. One must lose everything to gain everything.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Eighteen Upbuilding Discourses , Hong, One Who Prays Aright Struggles In Prayer and is Victorious-In That God is Victorious p. 380-381

text: The horrible or the absurd is uplifting, because it is only seemingly horrible or absurd.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: The Dionysian Vision of the World

text: If anyone could prove to me that Christ is outside the truth, and if the truth really did exclude Christ, I should prefer to stay with Christ and not with truth.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: Letter to Mme. N. D. Fonvisin (1854), as published in Letters of Fyodor Michailovitch Dostoevsky to his Family and Friends (1914), translated by Ethel Golburn Mayne, Letter XXI, p. 71

text: In the light, the earth remains our first and our last love. Our brothers are breathing under the same sky as we; justice is a living thing. Now is born that strange joy which helps one live and die, and which we shall never again postpone to a later time.
author: Albert Camus

text: An intellectual is someone whose mind watches itself.
author: Albert Camus

text: The Sophist demonstrates that everything is true and nothing is true.
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source_section: On the Concept of Irony with Continual Reference to Socrates (1841)

text: With the death of Greek tragedy, an immense, deeply felt void arose. A painful lament echoed through the Hellenic world: 'Tragedy is dead! Poetry itself has perished with her!' It was as if the shattering cry, 'Great Pan is dead!' had sounded once more.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Greek tragedy met her death in a different way from all the older sister arts: she died tragically by her own hand, after irresolvable conflicts, while the others died happy and peaceful at an advanced age. If a painless death, leaving behind beautiful progeny, is the sign of a happy natural state, then the endings of the other arts show us the example of just such a happy natural state: they sink slowly, and with their dying eyes they behold their fairer offspring, who lift up their heads in bold impatience. The death of Greek tragedy, on the other hand, left a great void whose effects were felt profoundly, far and wide; as once Greek sailors in Tiberius' time heard the distressing cry 'the god Pan is dead' issuing from a lonely island, now, throughout the Hellenic world, this cry resounded like an agonized lament: 'Tragedy is dead! Poetry itself died with it! Away, away with you, puny, stunted imitators! Away with you to Hades, and eat your fill of the old masters' crumbs!'

text: I conclude that all is well," says Oedipus , and that remark is sacred . It echoes in the wild and limited universe of man. It teaches that all is not, has not been, exhausted. It drives out of this world a god who had come into it with dissatisfaction and a preference for futile suffering. It makes of fate a human matter, which must be settled among men.
author: Albert Camus

text: Ironic philosophies produce passionate works. Any thought that abandons unity glorifies diversity! And diversity is the home of art. The only thought to liberate the mind is that which leaves it alone, certain of its limits and of its impending end. No doctrine tempts it. It awaits the ripening of the work and of life.
author: Albert Camus

text: Do you know that ages will pass and mankind will proclaim in its wisdom and science that there is no crime and, therefore no sin, but that there are only hungry people. "Feed them first and then demand virtue of them!" — that is what they will inscribe on their banner which they will raise against you and which will destroy your temple.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: The absurd man says yes and his effort will henceforth be unceasing.
author: Albert Camus

text: It is sometimes even good to be ridiculous, for we can the sooner forgive each other and humble ourselves. We cannot understand everything at once, nor begin with perfection; to achieve it, one must first begin by not understanding many things. If we understand too quickly, we may not understand well.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: The second half of a man's life is made up of nothing but the habits he has acquired during the first half.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: As quoted in Peter's Quotations: Ideas for Our Time (1979) by Laurence J. Peter, p. 299

text: Sisyphus is the absurd hero, as much through his passions as through his torture. His scorn of the gods, his hatred of death, and his passion for life won him that unspeakable penalty in which the whole being is exerted toward accomplishing nothing. This is the price that must be paid for the passions of this earth.
author: Albert Camus

text: To be an example to others: one who wishes to set a good example must add a grain of folly to their virtue. For then, people will imitate, and in doing so, feel elevated above the one they emulate—a delight humans cherish.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Model for others . He who wants to set a good example must add a grain of foolishness to his virtue; then others can imitate and, at the same time, rise above the one being imitated - something which people love.

text: One dies twice: to cease to live is nothing, but to cease to love and to be loved is an insupportable death.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: The Absurd Man, without negating the eternal, does nothing for it. Not that nostalgia is foreign to him, but he prefers his courage and his reasoning. The first teaches him to live without appeal and to get along with what he has; the second informs him of his limits. Assured of his temporally limited freedom, of his revolt devoid of future, and of his mortal consciousness, he lives out his adventure within the span of his lifetime.
author: Albert Camus

text: Whatever we may do, excess will always keep its place in the heart of man, in the place where solitude is found. We all carry within us our places of exile, our crimes and our ravages. But our task is not to unleash them on the world; it is to fight them in ourselves and in others.
author: Albert Camus

text: Free will without fate is as inconceivable as spirit without the real, or good without evil.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: Free will without fate is no more conceivable than spirit without matter, good without evil. "Fatum und Geschichte," April 1862

text: Humiliate the reason and distort the soul...
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Nobody realizes that some people expend tremendous energy merely to be normal.
author: Albert Camus

text: His fate belongs to him. His rock is his thing.
author: Albert Camus

text: Existence is illusory and it is eternal.
author: Albert Camus

text: People are scarcely aware that it is a slavery they are creating; they forget this in their zeal to make people free by overthrowing dominions. They are scarcely aware that it is slavery; how could it be possible to be a slave in relation to equals?
author: Soren Kierkegaard
source: Søren Kierkegaard, Upbuilding Discourses in Various Spirits , Hong p. 327

text: All truths are bloody truths to me.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Of all the schools of patience and lucidity, creation is the most effective. It is also the staggering evidence of man's sole dignity: the dogged revolt against his condition, perseverance in an effort considered sterile. It calls for a daily effort, self-mastery, a precise estimate of the limits of truth, measure, and strength. It constitutes an ascesis. All that "for nothing," in order to repeat and mark time. But perhaps the great work of art has less importance in itself than in the ordeal it demands of a man and the opportunity it provides him of overcoming his phantoms and approaching a little closer to his naked reality.
author: Albert Camus

text: Ivan is the incarnation of the refusal to be the only one saved. He throws in his lot with the damned and, for their sake, rejects eternity. If he had faith, he could, in fact, be saved, but others would be damned and suffering would continue. There is no possible salvation for the man who feels real compassion.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: Albert Camus, The Rebel , A. Bower, trans. (1956), pp. 56-57

text: Sometimes goldfinches one by one will drop From low hung branches; little space they stop; But sip, and twitter, and their feathers sleek; Then off at once, as in a wanton freak: Or perhaps, to show their black, and golden wings Pausing upon their yellow flutterings.
author: John Keats

text: My chest of books divide amongst my friends.
author: John Keats

text: Every mental pursuit takes its reality and worth from the ardour of the pursuer.
author: John Keats

text: The silver snarling trumpets 'gan to chide.
author: John Keats

text: To one who has been long in city pent, ’Tis very sweet to look into the fair And open face of heaven.
author: John Keats
source: "Sonnet. To One Who Has Been Long in City Pent"

text: It was a fatal day when the public discovered that the pen is mightier than the paving-stone, and can be made as offensive as the brickbat. For behind the leading-article lies prejudice, stupidity, cant, and twaddle, which, joined together, make a terrible force and constitute the new authority.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Most modern calendars mar the sweet simplicity of our lives by reminding us that each day that passes is the anniversary of some perfectly uninteresting event.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: "A New Calendar," The Pall Mall Gazette (February 17, 1887)

text: You are so young, so at the very beginning, and I would ask you, dear Sir, to have patience with all that is unresolved in your heart, and to try to love the questions themselves as if they were locked rooms or books written in a very foreign tongue. Do not seek the answers now, for they cannot be given to you, as you would not be able to live them. And the point is to live everything. Live the questions now. Perhaps then, gradually, without noticing it, you will live your way into the answer, one distant day.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Be patient toward all that is unsolved in your heart and try to love the questions themselves, like locked rooms and like books that are now written in a very foreign tongue. Do not now seek the answers, which cannot be given you because you would not be able to live them. And the point is, to live everything. Live the questions now. Perhaps you will then gradually, without noticing it, live along some distant day into the answer.

text: First, a childhood, boundless, without surrender or aim. O unconscious delight. Then, suddenly, terror, barrier, school, drudgery, and a fall into temptation and loss. Defiance. The bent one becomes the bender, avenging on others his own defeat. Loved, feared, rescuer, wrestler, victor, and overcomer, blow by blow. And then, alone in the vast, light, cold. Yet deep within the erected form, a breath drawn for the First, the Ancient... There, God fell from his ambush.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: First a childhood, limitless and without renunciation or goals. O unselfconscious joy. Then suddenly terror, barriers, schools, drudgery, and collapse into temptation and loss. Defiance. The one bent becomes the bender, and thrusts upon others that which it suffered. Loved, feared, rescuer, fighter, winner and conqueror, blow by blow. And then alone in cold, light, open space, yet still deep within the mature erected form, a gasping for the clear air of the first one, the old one... Then God leaps out from behind his hiding place.

text: This toil, to move heavily, as if bound, through what is yet undone, resembles the swan's uncreated gait. And dying, this letting go of the ground on which we daily stand, is like its anxious settling down — into the waters that gently receive it, and which, as if happy and past, withdraw beneath it, wave after wave; while it, infinitely still and sure, ever more mature, more kingly, and more serene, rests in its drawing.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: This difficult living, heavy and as if all tied up, moving through that which has been left undone, is like the not-quite-finished walk of the swan. And dying, this slipping away from the ground upon which we stand every day, is his anxious letting himself fall—: into the waters, which receive him gladly and which, as if happily already gone by, draw back under him, wave after wave; while the swan, infinitely calm and self-assured, opener and more magnificent and more serene, allows himself to be drawn on.

text: I compare human life to a large mansion of many apartments, two of which I can only describe, the doors of the rest being as yet shut upon me.
author: John Keats

text: He play'd an ancient ditty long since mute, In Provence call'd "La belle dame sans mercy."
author: John Keats

text: Just as the creative artist is not allowed to choose, neither is he permitted to turn his back on anything: a single refusal, and he is cast out of the state of grace and becomes sinful all the way through.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letter to his wife, reprinted in Rilke’s Letters on Cézanne (1952, trans. 1985). (October 23, 1907)

text: The grandeur of the dooms We have imagined for the mighty dead; All lovely tales that we have heard or read: An endless fountain of immortal drink, Pouring unto us from the heaven's brink.
author: John Keats

text: In spite of all, Some shape of beauty moves away the pall From our dark spirits.
author: John Keats

text: And still she slept an azure-lidded sleep, In blanched linen, smooth, and lavender'd.
author: John Keats

text: I have the kiss of Walt Whitman still on my lip.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: In a journal or later note by George Cecil Ives recording a meeting with Wilde in 1900, Oscar Wilde: Myths, Miracles and Imitations (Cambridge University Press,1996), John Stokes

text: Death is the side of life which is turned away from us.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: She dwells with Beauty — Beauty that must die; And Joy, whose hand is ever at his lips Bidding adieu; and aching Pleasure nigh, Turning to poison while the bee-mouth sips. Ay, in the very temple of Delight Veil'd Melancholy has her sovran shrine, Though seen of none save him whose strenuous tongue Can burst Joy's grape against his palate fine; His soul shall taste the sadness of her might, And be among her cloudy trophies hung.
author: John Keats

text: The great renewal of the world will perhaps consist in this, that man and maid, freed of all false feelings and reluctances, will seek each other not as opposites, but as brother and sister, as neighbors, and will come together as human beings.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: O, what can ail thee, knight-at-arms, Alone and palely loitering? The sedge has wither'd from the lake, And no birds sing.
author: John Keats

text: Just as language has no longer anything in common with the thing it names, so the movements of most of the people who live in cities have lost their connexion with the earth; they hang, as it were, in the air, hover in all directions, and find no place where they can settle.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Lovers... when you raise yourselves, mouth to mouth, drink to drink: how strangely then the drinker is lost to the act.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Lovers ... when you raise yourselves and press your mouths together—drink upon drink: strange how each of you drinks your way past the other.

text: There was an awful rainbow once in heaven: We know her woof, her texture; she is given In the dull catalogue of common things. Philosophy will clip an Angel's wings, Conquer all mysteries by rule and line, Empty the haunted air, and gnomed mine — Unweave a rainbow, as it erewhile made The tender-person'd Lamia melt into a shade.
author: John Keats

text: I have two luxuries to brood over in my walks, your loveliness and the hour of my death. O that I could have possession of them both in the same minute.
author: John Keats

text: I have been astonished that Men could die Martyrs for religion – I have shudder’d at it – I shudder no more – I could be martyr’d for my Religion – Love is my religion – I could die for that – I could die for you. My Creed is Love and you are its only tenet.
author: John Keats

text: Make your ego porous. Will is of little importance, complaining is nothing, fame is nothing. Openness, patience, receptivity, solitude is everything.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: As quoted in Sunbeams : A Book of Quotations (1990) by Sy Safransky, p. 42

text: They more adeptly bend the willow's branches who have experience of the willow's roots.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Pleasure is oft a visitant; but pain Clings cruelly to us.
author: John Keats

text: What is required of us is that we love the difficult and learn to deal with it. In the difficult are the friendly forces, the hands that work on us. Right in the difficult we must have our joys, our happiness, our dreams: there against the depth of this background, they stand out, there for the first time we see how beautiful they are.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Selected Letters of Rainer Maria Rilke (1960)

text: Who are these coming to the sacrifice? To what green altar, O mysterious priest, Lead'st thou that heifer lowing at the skies, And all her silken flanks with garlands drest?
author: John Keats

text: Thou, silent form, dost tease us out of thought As doth eternity: Cold Pastoral! When old age shall this generation waste, Thou shalt remain, in midst of other woe Than ours, a friend to man, to whom thou say'st, "Beauty is truth, truth beauty," — that is all Ye know on earth, and all ye need to know.
author: John Keats
source: Stanza 5. The final lines of this poem have been rendered in various ways in different editions, some placing the entire last two lines within quotation marks, others only the statement " Beauty is truth, truth beauty ," and others without any quotation marks. The poet's final intentions upon the matter before his death are unclear.

text: Whoever hears me assert that the grey cat playing just now in the yard is the same one that did jumps and tricks there five hundred years ago will think whatever he likes of me, but it is a stranger form of madness to imagine that the present-day cat is fundamentally an entirely different one.
author: John Keats
source: Jorge Luis Borges in "A History of Eternity" as translated in Selected Non-Fictions Vol. 1, (1999), edited by Eliot Weinberger

text: Unclasps her warmed jewels one by one; Loosens her fragrant bodice; by degrees Her rich attire creeps rustling to her knees.
author: John Keats

text: I cannot see what flowers are at my feet, Nor what soft incense hangs upon the boughs, But, in embalmed darkness, guess each sweet.
author: John Keats

text: Nought but a lovely sighing of the wind Along the reedy stream; a half-heard strain, Full of sweet desolation—balmy pain.
author: John Keats

text: There is not a fiercer hell than the failure in a great object.
author: John Keats

text: Music's golden tongue Flatter'd to tears this aged man and poor.
author: John Keats

text: O, for a draught of vintage! that hath been Cool'd a long age in the deep-delved earth. Tasting of Flora and the country green, Dance, and Provencal song, and sunburnt mirth! O for a beaker full of the warm South, Full of the true, the blushful Hippocrene, With beaded bubbles winking at the brim, And purple-stained mouth.
author: John Keats

text: And there shall be for thee all soft delight That shadowy thought can win, A bright torch, and a casement ope at night, To let the warm Love in!
author: John Keats

text: Painting is something that takes place among the colors; one must leave them alone completely, so they can settle the matter among themselves. Their intercourse: this is the whole of painting.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letter to his wife, reprinted in Rilke’s Letters on Cézanne (1952, trans. 1985). (October 21, 1907)

text: He [Cézanne] reproduced himself with so much humble objectivity, with the unquestioning, matter of fact interest of a dog who sees himself in a mirror and thinks: there's another dog.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letter to his wife, reprinted in Rilke’s Letters on Cézanne (1952, trans. 1985). (October 23, 1907)

text: But strength alone though of the Muses born Is like a fallen angel: trees uptorn, Darkness , and worms , and shrouds, and sepulchres Delight it; for it feeds upon the burrs, And thorns of life; forgetting the great end Of poesy, that it should be a friend To sooth the cares, and lift the thoughts of man.
author: John Keats

text: But when the melancholy fit shall fall Sudden from heaven like a weeping cloud, That fosters the droop-headed flowers all, And hides the green hill in an April shroud; Then glut thy sorrow on a morning rose.
author: John Keats

text: My restless spirit never could endure To brood so long upon one luxury, Unless it did, though fearfully, espy A hope beyond the shadow of a dream.
author: John Keats

text: I could give you no advice but this: to go into yourself and to explore the depths where your life wells forth.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Forlorn! the very word is like a bell To toil me back from thee to my sole self!
author: John Keats

text: No experience has been too unimportant, and the smallest event unfolds like a fate, and fate itself is like a wonderful, wide fabric in which every thread is guided by an infinitely tender hand and laid alongside another thread and is held and supported by a hundred others.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Then in a wailful choir the small gnats mourn Among the river sallows, borne aloft Or sinking as the light wind lives or dies.
author: John Keats

text: Call the world if you please "The vale of soul-making."
author: John Keats
source: To George and Georgiana Keats (21 April 1819)

text: A poet is the most unpoetical of anything in existence; because he has no identity — he is continually informing — and filling some other body.
author: John Keats

text: True myth serves as an inexhaustible source of intellectual speculation, religious joy, ethical inquiry, and artistic renewal. The real mystery is not destroyed by reason; the fake one vanishes. When true myth rises into consciousness, its message is always: You must change your life.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Ursula Le Guin , "Myth and Archetype in Science Fiction" (1976)

text: Shed no tear! O shed no tear! The flower will bloom another year. Weep no more! O weep no more! Young buds sleep in the root's white core.
author: John Keats

text: Let the magnificent solitude work upon you, for it will become an indelible influence, shaping you as profoundly as the blood of your ancestors.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: 'Tis the pest Of love, that fairest joys give most unrest.
author: John Keats

text: I seek happiness only in the present hour; nothing startles me beyond the moment. The setting sun always sets me right, and a sparrow at my window invites me into its existence.
author: John Keats

text: We cannot know his legendary head with eyes like ripening fruit. And yet his torso is still suffused with brilliance from inside, like a lamp, in which his gaze, now turned to low, gleams in all its power.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Archaic Torso of Apollo · Full text online

text: A tree ascended there. Oh pure transendence! Oh Orpheus sings! Oh tall tree in the ear! And all things hushed. Yet even in that silence a new beginning, beckoning, change appeared.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Scenery is fine — but human nature is finer.
author: John Keats

text: Ah, you are the gardens I beheld with such hope. An open window in the country house, and you almost approached me, lost in thought. I found alleys you had just walked, and the merchants' mirrors, still dizzy from your presence, startled me with my sudden image. Who knows if the same bird did not echo through us yesterday, alone, in the evening?
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: You, Beloved, who are all the gardens I have ever gazed at, longing. An open window in a country house —, and you almost stepped out, pensive, to meet me. Streets that I chanced upon,— you had just walked down them and vanished. And sometimes, in a shop, the mirrors were still dizzy with your presence and, startled, gave back my too-sudden image. Who knows? perhaps the same bird echoed through both of us yesterday, separate, in the evening...

text: Bards of Passion and of Mirth, Ye have left your souls on earth! Have ye souls in heaven too, Double-lived in regions new?
author: John Keats
source: "Ode: The Fair Maid of the Inn"

text: If I should die, having left no immortal work, know that I loved the principle of beauty in all things, and with time, I would have made myself remembered.
author: John Keats

text: The demands of love's difficult work upon our development are immense, and as beginners, we are not up to them. Yet, if we embrace love as a burden and apprenticeship, a little progress and alleviation may be perceptible to those who come long after us.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Stop and consider! life is but a day; A fragile dew-drop on its perilous way From a tree's summit.
author: John Keats

text: When I have fears that I may cease to be Before my pen has glean'd my teeming brain, Before high piled books, in charact'ry, Hold like rich garners the full-ripen'd grain; When I behold, upon the night's starr'd face, Huge cloudy symbols of a high romance, And think that I may never live to trace Their shadows, with the magic hand of chance; And when I feel, fair creature of an hour! That I shall never look upon thee more, Never have relish in the faery power Of unreflecting love! — then on the shore Of the wide world I stand alone, and think Till Love and Fame to nothingness do sink.
author: John Keats
source: " When I have fears that I may cease to be " (1817)

text: Physical pleasure is a sensual experience, a great unending knowing of the world, the fullness and glory of all knowing. Yet, most misuse and squander it, applying it as a mere stimulant or distraction, rather than a rallying toward exalted moments.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: And other spirits there are standing apart Upon the forehead of the age to come; These, these will give the world another heart, And other pulses. Hear ye not the hum Of mighty workings in a distant mart? Listen awhile ye nations, and be dumb.
author: John Keats
source: "Sonnet. Addressed to the Same" (Benjamin Robert Haydon)

text: E’en like the passage of an angel’s tear That falls through the clear ether silently.
author: John Keats
source: "Sonnet. To One Who Has Been Long in City Pent"

text: How shall I hold my soul, that it may not touch yours? How shall I lift it above you, to other things? Oh, gladly would I shelter it with something lost in the dark, in a strange, still place that vibrates not when your depths vibrate. Yet all that touches us, you and me, draws us together like a bowstroke, pulling one voice from two strings. Upon what instrument are we strung? And which violinist holds us in his hand? O sweet song.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: How shall I hold on to my soul, so that it does not touch yours? How shall I lift it gently up over you on to other things? I would so very much like to tuck it away among long lost objects in the dark, in some quiet, unknown place, somewhere which remains motionless when your depths resound. And yet everything which touches us, you and me, takes us together like a single bow, drawing out from two strings but one voice. On which instrument are we strung? And which violinist holds us in his hand? O sweetest of songs.

text: You, beloved, lost in advance, never-arrived, I know not what tones are dear to you. No longer do I try to recognize you when the future surges.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: You who never arrived in my arms, Beloved, who were lost from the start, I don't even know what songs would please you. I have given up trying to recognize you in the surging wave of the next moment.

text: Full on this casement shone the wintry moon, And threw warm gules on Madeline's fair breast , As down she knelt for heaven's grace and boon; Rose-bloom fell on her hands, together prest.
author: John Keats

text: Negative capability, that is, when a man is capable of being in uncertainties, mysteries, doubts, without any irritable reaching after fact and reason.
author: John Keats
source: To George and Thomas Keats (22 December 1817)

text: The day is gone, and all its sweets are gone! Sweet voice, sweet lips, soft hand, and softer breast.
author: John Keats

text: Who, if I cried out, would hear me among the angelic orders? And even if one of them suddenly took me to his heart, I would vanish in his stronger existence. For beauty is nothing but the beginning of terror, which we are still just able to endure, and we admire it so because it calmly disdains to destroy us. Every angel is terrifying.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Who, if I cried out, would hear me among the angels' hierarchies? and even if one of them pressed me against his heart: I would be consumed in that overwhelming existence. For beauty is nothing but the beginning of terror, which we still are just able to endure, and we are so awed because it serenely disdains to annihilate us. Every angel is terrifying.

text: If your daily life seems poor, do not blame it; blame yourself, tell yourself that you are not poet enough to call forth its riches; for to the creator there is no poverty and no poor indifferent place.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letter One (17 February 1903) as translated by M. D. Herter Norton (1993)

text: You are always new. The last of your kisses was ever the sweetest; the last smile the brightest; the last movement the gracefullest.
author: John Keats

text: O magic sleep! O comfortable bird, That broodest o'er the troubled sea of the mind Till it is hush'd and smooth!
author: John Keats

text: I am too alone in the world, and yet not alone enough to consecrate every hour. I am too small in the world, and yet not small enough to be before you like a thing, dark and wise. I want my will and want my will to accompany the paths to action; and in quiet, somehow hesitant times, when something approaches, I want to be among the knowing or alone.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: I am too alone in the world, and yet not alone enough to make every hour holy. I am too small in the world, and yet not tiny enough just to stand before you like a thing, dark and shrewd. I want my will, and I want to be with my will as it moves towards deed; and in those quiet, somehow hesitating times, when something is approaching, I want to be with those who are wise or else alone. Number 2 (as translated by Cliff Crego)

text: Poetry should be great and unobtrusive, a thing which enters into one's soul, and does not startle it or amaze it with itself, but with its subject.
author: John Keats

text: So let me be thy choir, and make a moan Upon the midnight hours
author: John Keats

text: I stood tip-toe upon a little hill, The air was cooling, and so very still, That the sweet buds which with a modest pride Pull droopingly, in slanting curve aside, Their scantly leaved, and finely tapering stems, Had not yet lost those starry diadems Caught from the early sobbing of the morn.
author: John Keats

text: A work of art is good if it has grown out of necessity.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Works of art are of an infinite solitude, and no means of approach is so useless as criticism.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Sweet are the pleasures that to verse belong, And doubly sweet a brotherhood in song.
author: John Keats

text: St. Agnes' Eve — Ah, bitter chill it was! The owl, for all his feathers, was a-cold; The hare limp'd trembling through the frozen grass, And silent was the flock in woolly fold.
author: John Keats

text: Axioms in philosophy are not axioms until they are proved upon our pulses: we read fine things but never feel them to the full until we have gone the same steps as the author.
author: John Keats

text: A thing of beauty is a joy forever: Its loveliness increases; it will never Pass into nothingness ; but still will keep A bower quiet for us, and a sleep Full of sweet dreams, and health, and quiet breathing.
author: John Keats

text: Who hath not seen thee oft amid thy store? Sometimes whoever seeks abroad may find Thee sitting careless on a granary floor, Thy hair soft-lifted by the winnowing wind; Or on a half-reap'd furrow sound asleep, Drows'd with the fume of poppies, while thy hook Spares the next swath and all its twined flowers.
author: John Keats

text: The early departed no longer need us; they gently wean themselves from the earthly, as one outgrows a mother's breast. But we, who crave great mysteries, from whose sorrow often springs blessed progress—could we truly exist without them?
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: In the end, those who were carried off early no longer need us: they are weaned from earth's sorrows and joys, and as gently as children outgrow the soft breasts of their mothers. But we, who do need such great mysteries, we for whom grief is so often the source of our spirit's growth — : could we exist without them ?

text: The longer I live, the more imperative it seems to transcribe life's full dictation to its very end; for perhaps only the final sentence holds that small, inconspicuous word through which all that was laboriously learned and uncomprehended transforms into glorious meaning.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: The longer I live, the more urgent it seems to me to endure and transcribe the whole dictation of existence up to its end, for it might just be the case that only the very last sentence contains that small and possibly inconspicuous word through which everything we had struggled to learn and everything we had failed to understand will be transformed suddenly into magnificent sense. Letter to Ilse Erdmann, 21 December 1913, in Letters on Life , U. Baer, trans. (2007)

text: Works of genius are the first things in this world.
author: John Keats
source: To G. and F. Keats (13 January 1818)

text: Is it possible that people say 'God' and mean something common? Consider two identical knives, bought on the same day, which after a week barely resemble each other, so differently have they developed in different hands. Is it possible to believe one can have a God without using Him?
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Is it possible that there are people who say "God" and suppose they mean something shared by all? — Only consider two schoolboys: one of them buys a knife, and the other buys an identical one on the same day. And a week later, they show each other the two knives, and they turn out to be only remotely similar, so differently have they been shaped by different hands. ... Is it possible to believe we could have a god without making use of him?
source_section: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge (1910)

text: Love in a hut, with water and a crust, Is — Love, forgive us! — cinders, ashes, dust.
author: John Keats

text: As when, upon a tranced summer-night, Those green-rob'd senators of mighty woods, Tall oaks , branch-charmed by the earnest stars, Dream, and so dream all night without a stir, Save from one gradual solitary gust Which comes upon the silence, and dies off, As if the ebbing air had but one wave.
author: John Keats

text: Almost everything serious is difficult, and everything is serious.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Alexandra Stoddard in Roxanne J. Coady and Joy Johannessen (eds.) The Book That Changed My Life (2006), ISBN 1-592-40210-0 , p. 166

text: You might curb your magnanimity, and be more of an artist, and load every rift of your subject with ore.
author: John Keats

text: They will explain themselves — as all poems should do without any comment.
author: John Keats

text: Love is at first not anything that means merging, giving over and uniting with another; it is a high inducement to the individual to ripen, to become world, to become world for himself for another's sake. It is a great exacting claim upon him, something that chooses him out and calls him to vast things.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Now comes the pain of truth , to whom 'tis pain; O folly! for to bear all naked truths, And to envisage circumstance, all calm, That is the top of sovereignty Mark well.
author: John Keats

text: Art too is just a way of living, and however one lives, one can, without knowing, prepare for it; in everything real one is closer to it, more its neighbor, than in the unreal half-artistic professions, which, while they pretend to be close to art, in practice deny and attack the existence of all art.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Open afresh your round of starry folds, Ye ardent marigolds!
author: John Keats

text: And mid-May's eldest child, The coming musk-rose, full of dewy wine, The murmurous haunt of flies on summer eves.
author: John Keats

text: As though a rose should shut and be a bud again.
author: John Keats

text: A man's life of any worth is a continual allegory, a life like the Scriptures, figurative. Very few eyes can see its mystery. Shakespeare led a life of allegory: his works are the comments on it.
author: John Keats
source: To George and Georgiana Keats (14 February – 3 May 1819)

text: My heart aches, and a drowsy numbness pains My sense, as though of hemlock I had drunk, Or emptied some dull opiate to the drains One minute past, and Lethe-wards had sunk: 'Tis not through envy of thy happy lot, But being too happy in thine happiness, — That thou, light-winged Dryad of the trees, In some melodious plot Of beechen green, and shadows numberless, Singest of summer in full-throated ease.
author: John Keats

text: Bright star! would I were stedfast as thou art — Not in lone splendour hung aloft the night And watching with eternal lids apart, Like nature's patient, sleepless Eremite, The moving waters at their priestlike task Of pure ablution round earth's human shores.
author: John Keats

text:
Blind me: I still see you.
Seal my ears: I still hear you.
Footless, I can reach you.
Mouthless, I can still invoke you.
Break my arms: I hold you with my heart as with a hand.
Stop my heart: my brain will beat.
And if you cast fire into my brain,
I will carry you upon my blood.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: There is an awful warmth about my heart like a load of immortality.
author: John Keats

text: This living hand, now warm and capable Of earnest grasping, would, if it were cold And in the icy silence of the tomb, So haunt thy days and chill thy dreaming nights That thou wouldst wish thine own heart dry of blood So in my veins red life might stream again, And thou be conscience-calm'd — see here it is — I hold it towards you.
author: John Keats

text: Almost any man may, like the spider, spin from his own inwards his own citadel.
author: John Keats

text: Time, that aged nurse, Rocked me to patience.
author: John Keats

text: She sighed for Agnes’ dreams, the sweetest of the year.
author: John Keats

text:
My gaze already races to the sunlit hill,
ahead of the path I've barely begun.
Thus, what we could not grasp,
full of presence, seizes us from afar—
and transforms us, though we never reach it,
into that which, barely sensing, we are;
a sign unfurls, answering our own...
Yet we feel only the headwind.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Already my gaze is upon the hill, the sunny one, at the end of the path which I've only just begun. So we are grasped, by that which we could not grasp, at such great distance, so fully manifest— and it changes us, even when we do not reach it, into something that, hardly sensing it, we already are; a sign appears, echoing our own sign... But what we sense is the falling winds.

text: In drear-nighted December, Too happy, happy tree, Thy branches ne'er remember Their green felicity.
author: John Keats

text: Souls of Poets dead and gone, What Elysium have ye known, Happy field or mossy cavern, Choicer than the Mermaid Tavern?
author: John Keats
source: " Lines on the Mermaid Tavern ", ll. 1–4

text: Thou wast not born for death, immortal Bird! No hungry generations tread thee down; The voice I hear this passing night was heard In ancient days by emperor and clown : Perhaps the self-same song that found a path Through the sad heart of Ruth, when, sick for home, She stood in tears amid the alien corn; The same that oft-times hath Charm'd magic casements, opening on the foam Of perilous seas, in faery lands forlorn.
author: John Keats

text: Darkling I listen; and, for many a time I have been half in love with easeful Death , Call'd him soft names in many a musèd rhyme, To take into the air my quiet breath; Now more than ever seems it rich to die, To cease upon the midnight with no pain, While thou art pouring forth thy soul abroad In such an ecstasy! Still wouldst thou sing, and I have ears in vain — To thy high requiem become a sod.
author: John Keats

text: But were there ever any Writh'd not of passed joy? The feel of not to feel it, When there is none to heal it, Nor numbed sense to steel it, Was never said in rhyme.
author: John Keats

text: Ever let the Fancy roam, Pleasure never is at home.
author: John Keats

text: I saw pale kings and princes too, Pale warriors, death-pale were they all; They cried- "La Belle Dame sans Merci Hath thee in thrall!"
author: John Keats

text: I want to warn and object: Let the things be! I enjoy listening to the sound they are making. But you always touch: and they hush and stand still. That's how you kill.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: The next tide erases the path in the mudflats, and all becomes alike on every side; but the small island out there has its eyes closed. Confusingly, the dike circles its inhabitants, who are born into a sleep where they silently mistake many worlds, for they rarely speak, and every sentence is like an epitaph.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: The next tide will erase the way through the mudflats, and everything will be again equal on all sides; but the small, far-out island already has its eyes closed; bewildered, the dike draws a circle around its inhabitants who were born into a sleep in which many worlds are silently confused, for they rarely speak, and every phrase is like an epitaph.

text: Someday there will be girls and women whose name will no longer mean the mere opposite of the male, but something in itself, something that makes one think not of any complement and limit, but only life and reality: the female human being.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: From infinite longings rise finite deeds, like weak fountains that early and trembling bow. But our joyful forces, which otherwise conceal themselves from us, reveal themselves in these dancing tears.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Out of infinite longings rise finite deeds like weak fountains, falling back just in time and trembling. And yet, what otherwise remains silent, our happy energies—show themselves in these dancing tears.
source_section: Das Buch der Bilder (The Book of Images) (1902)

text: If you trust in Nature, in the small things that can suddenly become huge; if you love what is humble and simply win the confidence of what seems poor: then everything will become easier, more coherent, and more reconciling, not in your conscious mind, but in your innermost awareness, awakeness, and knowledge.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: O for a life of Sensations rather than of Thoughts!
author: John Keats

text: To his sight The husk of natural objects opens quite To the core; and every secret essence there Reveals the elements of good and fair; Making him see, where Learning hath no light.
author: John Keats

text: O for ten years, that I may overwhelm Myself in poesy; so I may do the deed That my own soul has to itself decreed.
author: John Keats

text: Wherein lies happiness? In that which becks Our ready minds to fellowship divine, A fellowship with essence; till we shine, Full alchemiz'd, and free of space. Behold The clear religion of heaven!
author: John Keats

text: Was it a vision, or a waking dream? Fled is that music: — Do I wake or sleep?
author: John Keats

text: A drainless shower Of light is poesy; ’tis the supreme of power; ’Tis might half slumb’ring on its own right arm.
author: John Keats

text: His gaze, from the passing of the bars, has grown so weary that it holds nothing more. To him, it is as if there were a thousand bars, and behind a thousand bars, no world. The soft tread of supple, strong steps, which turns in the smallest circle, is like a dance of strength around a center, in which a great will stands stunned. Only sometimes the curtain of the pupil slides silently open—. Then an image enters, goes through the limbs' tense stillness— and ceases to be in the heart.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: His tired gaze - from passing endless bars - has turned into a vacant stare which nothing holds. To him there seem to be a thousand bars, and out beyond these bars exists no world. His supple gait, the smoothness of strong strides that gently turn in ever smaller circles perform a dance of strength, centered deep within a will, stunned, but untamed, indomitable. But sometimes the curtains of his eyelids part, the pupils of his eyes dilate as images of past encounters enter while through his limbs a tension strains in silence only to cease to be, to die within his heart.

text: Lo! with a little rod I did but touch the honey of romance — And must I lose a soul's inheritance?
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Nothing touches a work of art so little as words of criticism. Most experiences are unsayable, happening in a space no word has ever entered; and more unsayable than all other things are works of art, those mysterious existences whose life endures beside our own small, transitory life.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Thou still unravish'd bride of quietness, Thou foster-child of silence and slow time, Sylvan historian, who canst thus express A flowery tale more sweetly than our rhyme: What leaf-fringed legend haunts about thy shape? Of deities or mortals, or of both, In Tempe or the dales of Arcady? What men or gods are these? What maidens loth? What mad pursuit? What struggle to escape? What pipes and timbrels? What wild ecstasy?
author: John Keats

text: He ne'er is crown'd With immortality, who fears to follow Where airy voices lead.
author: John Keats

text: The passion poesy, glories infinite, haunt us till they become a cheering light unto our souls, and bound to us so fast that, whether there be shine or gloom o'ercast, they always must be with us, or we die.
author: John Keats

text: None can usurp this height... But those to whom the miseries of the world Are misery, and will not let them rest.
author: John Keats
source: " The Fall of Hyperion: A Dream " (1819), Canto I, l. 147

text: Find in yourself enough patience to endure and enough simplicity to have faith; gain more and more confidence in what is difficult and in your solitude among other people. And as for the rest, let life happen to you. Believe me: life is in the right, always.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: As bees gather honey, so we collect what is sweetest out of all things and build Him. Even with the trivial, with the insignificant (as long as it is done out of love), we begin Him whom we will not live to see. Is there anything that can deprive you of the hope that in this way you will someday exist in Him, who is the farthest, the outermost limit?
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Heard melodies are sweet, but those unheard Are sweeter: therefore, ye soft pipes, play on; Not to the sensual ear, but, more endear'd, Pipe to the spirit ditties of no tone. Fair youth, beneath the trees, thou canst not leave Thy song, nor ever can those trees be bare; Bold Lover, never, never canst thou kiss, Though winning near the goal — yet, do not grieve; She cannot fade, though thou hast not thy bliss, For ever wilt thou love, and she be fair!
author: John Keats

text: Otherwise this stone would seem defaced beneath the translucent cascade of the shoulders and would not glisten like a wild beast's fur: would not, from all the borders of itself, burst like a star: for here there is no place that does not see you. You must change your life.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: The poetical character is not itself — it has no self — it is every thing and nothing. What shocks the virtuous philosopher, delights the chameleon poet.
author: John Keats

text: They must not forget, when they love, that they are beginners, bunglers of life, apprentices in love—must learn love, and that like all learning wants peace, patience, and composure.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: On young couples who have not yet matured enough to recognize and respect each other's solitude

text: Rose, oh pure contradiction, joy, to be no one's sleep under so many eyelids.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Translation: Rose, oh pure contradiction, desire, To be no one's sleep under so many Lids.

text: Sex is difficult; almost everything serious is difficult; and everything is serious. If you achieve a wholly individual relation to sex, one not influenced by convention and custom, then you will no longer have to be afraid of losing yourself and becoming unworthy of your dearest possession.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Almost all our sadnesses are moments of tension, felt as paralysis because we no longer hear our astonished emotions living. We change, as a house changes when a guest enters. The future enters us in this way, to be transformed within us, long before it happens. The quieter, more patient, and open we are in our sadnesses, the more deeply the new presence can enter us, and the more it becomes our fate.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: He destroyed the poetry of the rainbow by reducing it to a prism.
author: John Keats

text: Everywhere I am folded, there I am a lie.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: As quoted in News of the Universe : Poems of Twofold Consciousness (1995) by Robert Bly , p. 125

text: Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness, Close bosom-friend of the maturing sun; Conspiring with him how to load and bless With fruit the vines that round the thatch-eves run ; To bend with apples the moss'd cottage-trees, And fill all fruit with ripeness to the core; To swell the ground, and plump the hazel shells With a sweet kernel; to set budding more, And still more, later flowers for the bees, Until they think warm days will never cease, For Summer has o'er-brimm'd their clammy cells.
author: John Keats

text: I met a lady in the meads, Full beautiful — a faery's child, Her hair was long, her foot was light, And her eyes were wild.
author: John Keats

text: We must hold to what is difficult. It is good to be solitary, for solitude is difficult; that something is difficult must be a reason the more for us to do it. To love is good, too: love being difficult. For one human being to love another: that is perhaps the most difficult of all our tasks, the ultimate, the last test and proof, the work for which all other work is but preparation.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: It keeps eternal whisperings around Desolate shores, and with its mighty swell Gluts twice ten thousand Caverns, till the spell Of Hecate leaves them their old shadowy sound.
author: John Keats

text: Ideally, an artist should not become conscious of his insights; all his progress should enter so swiftly into the work that he is unable to recognise them in the moment of transition. The artist who waits, watching, detaining them, will find them transformed like the beautiful gold in the fairy tale which cannot remain gold because some small detail was not taken care of.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letter to his wife, reprinted in Rilke’s Letters on Cézanne (1952, trans. 1985). (October 21, 1907)

text: Why, you might read two sonnets, ere they reach To where the hurrying freshnesses aye preach A natural sermon o'er their pebbly beds; Where swarms of minnows show their little heads, Staying their wavy bodies ’gainst the streams , To taste the luxury of sunny beams Temper’d with coolness.
author: John Keats

text: All the soarings of my mind begin in my blood.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Wartime Letters of Rainer Maria Rilke 1914-1921 (1940), translated by M.D. Herter Norton

text: I think I shall be among the English Poets after my death.
author: John Keats
source: To George and Georgiana Keats (14 October 1818)

text: Lord: it is time. The summer was immense. Lay your shadow upon the sundials, and on the fields, unleash the winds.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Lord: it is time. The summer was immense. Let thine shadows upon the sundials fall, and unleash the winds upon the open fields.
source_section: Das Buch der Bilder (The Book of Images) (1902)

text: If only we arrange our life according to that principle which counsels us that we must hold to the difficult, then that which now still seems to us the most alien will become what we most trust and find most faithful.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Through such impressions, one gathers oneself, winning oneself back from the exacting multiplicity, and slowly learns to recognize the very few Things in which something eternal endures that one can love and something solitary that one can gently take part in.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: It is to do nothing that the elect exist. Action is limited and relative. Unlimited and absolute is the vision of him who sits at ease and watches, who walks in loneliness and dreams .
author: Oscar Wilde

text: All feelings that concentrate you and lift you up are pure; only that feeling is impure which grasps just one side of your being and thus distorts you. Every intensification is good, if it is in your entire blood, if it isn't intoxication or muddiness, but joy which you can see into, clear to the bottom.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Surely all art is the result of one's having been in danger, of having gone through an experience all the way to the end, where no one can go any further.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letter to his wife, reprinted in Rilke’s Letters on Cézanne (1952, trans. 1985). (June 24, 1907)

text: Irony: Do not let yourself be governed by it, especially not in unproductive moments. In productive ones try to make use of it as one more means of seizing life.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Yes, the springs truly needed you. Some stars assigned you to feel them. A wave rose from the past, or as you passed an open window, a violin surrendered itself. All this was a task. But did you master it? Were you not always scattered by expectation, as if everything announced a beloved?
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Yes—the springtimes needed you. Often a star was waiting for you to notice it. A wave rolled toward you out of the distant past, or as you walked under an open window, a violin yielded itself to your hearing. All this was mission. But could you accomplish it? Weren't you always distracted by expectation, as if every event announced a beloved? (Where can you find a place to keep her, with all the huge strange thoughts inside you going and coming and often staying all night.)

text: The evening slowly changes its robes, held for it by a fringe of ancient trees.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Slowly the evening changes into the clothes held for it by a row of ancient trees.
source_section: Das Buch der Bilder (The Book of Images) (1902)

text: The work of the eyes is done. Go now and do the heart-work on the images imprisoned within you.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Wendung (Turning Point) , as translated by Stephen Mitchell

text: My God, it burst upon me: so this is your truth. Proofs of your existence abound. I had forgotten them all, never sought them, for what immense obligation would lie in your certainty. And yet, now it is revealed.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: A thought struck me abruptly: My God, you do exist , then. There are proofs of Your existence. I had forgotten them all, and never demanded any either, for what an overwhelming obligation would come with this certainty. And yet that is what is now being shown to me.
source_section: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge (1910)

text: No one can advise or help you — no one. There is only one thing you should do. Go into yourself. Find out the reason that commands you to write; see whether it has spread its roots into the very depths of your heart; confess to yourself whether you would have to die if you were forbidden to write.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: He was a poet and hated the approximate.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: The Journal of My Other Self

text: Love is more difficult than other things, because in other conflicts nature herself enjoins men to collect themselves, to take themselves firmly in the hand with all their strength, while in the heightening of love the impulse is to give oneself wholly away.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: I can control my thoughts as necessary; then how can I be troubled?
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Only a very few people are artists in life; the art of life is the most distinguished and rarest of all the arts.
author: Carl Jung
source: Modern Man in Search of a Soul. Section - The Stages of Life (1933)
source_section: Modern Man in Search of a Soul (1933)

text: To be like the rock that the waves keep crashing over. It stands unmoved and the raging of the sea falls still around it.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The controlling Intelligence understands its own nature, and what it does, and whereon it works.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Prize that which is best in the universe; and this is that which useth everything and ordereth everything.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Learn your theories as well as you can, but put them aside when you touch the miracle of the living soul. Not theories, but your own creative individuality alone must decide.
author: Carl Jung

text: Do everything as if it were the last thing you were doing in your life, and stop being aimless, stop letting your emotions override what your mind tells you, stop being hypocritical, self-centered, irritable.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: You will find rest from vain fancies if you perform every act in life as though it were your last.

text: How much time he gains who does not look to see what his neighbor says or does or thinks, but only at what he does himself, to make it just and holy.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The things which will be are of like form, and cannot deviate from the order of things now. To have contemplated human life for forty years is the same as to have contemplated it for ten thousand years.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: ...undefiled by pleasures, invulnerable to any pain, untouched by arrogance, unaffected by meanness, an athlete in the greatest of all contests—the struggle not to be overwhelmed by anything that happens.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: We are so captivated by and entangled in our subjective consciousness that we have forgotten the age-old fact that God speaks chiefly through dreams and visions.
author: Carl Jung
source: The Symbolic Life (1953); also in Man and His Symbols (1964)

text: Nature must not win the game, but she cannot lose. Whenever the conscious mind clings to hard and fast concepts and gets caught in its own rules and regulations, nature pops up with her inescapable demands.
author: Carl Jung

text: Every civilized human being, whatever his conscious development, is still an archaic man at the deeper levels of his psyche.
author: Carl Jung
source_section: Modern Man in Search of a Soul (1933)

text: The ruling power within, when it is in its natural state, is so related to outer circumstances that it easily changes to accord with what can be done and what is given it to do.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The body and its parts are a river, the soul a dream and mist, life is warfare and a journey far from home, lasting reputation is oblivion.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: We are living in what the Greeks called the right time for a "metamorphosis of the gods," i.e., of the fundamental principles and symbols.
author: Carl Jung

text: Then what should we work for? Only this: proper understanding; unselfish action; truthful speech. A resolve to accept whatever happens as necessary and familiar, flowing like water from that same source and spring.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: We can never legitimately cut loose from our archetypal foundations unless we are prepared to pay the price of a neurosis, any more than we can rid ourselves of our body and its organs without committing suicide.
author: Carl Jung
source: J.B. Priestley, Times Literary Supplement , London (August 6, 1954)

text: You don't love yourself enough. Or you'd love your nature too, and what it demands of you.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The great decisions of human life have as a rule far more to do with the instincts and other mysterious unconscious factors than with conscious will and well-meaning reasonableness. The shoe that fits one person pinches another; there is no recipe for living that suits all cases. Each of us carries his own life-form—an indeterminable form which cannot be superseded by any other.
author: Carl Jung
source_section: Modern Man in Search of a Soul (1933)

text: One thing you must know, the one thing I have learned is that one must live this life. This life is the way, the long sought-after way to the unfathomable, which we call "divine". There is no other way. All other ways are false paths.
author: Carl Jung

text: 'Thinking is difficult. Therefore, let the herd pronounce judgement.'
author: Carl Jung
source: Frequently misquoted as "Thinking is difficult, that's why most people judge" and close variants.

text: Yet living and dying, honour and dishonour, pain and pleasure, riches and poverty, and so forth are equally the lot of good men and bad. Things like these neither elevate nor degrade; and therefore they are no more good than they are evil.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: I therefore consider it wiser to acknowledge the idea of God consciously, for, if we do not, something else is made God, usually something quite inappropriate and stupid such as only an "enlightened" intellect could hatch forth.
author: Carl Jung
source: C. G. Jung. 2014. Collected Works of C.G. Jung, Volume 7: Two Essays in Analytical Psychology . Princeton University Press. p. 71

text: All that is harmony for you, my Universe, is in harmony with me as well. Nothing that comes at the right time for you is too early or too late for me. Everything is fruit to me that your seasons bring, Nature. All things come of you, have their being in you, and return to you.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: Πᾶν μοι συναρμόζει ὃ σοὶ εὐάρμοστόν ἐστιν, ὦ κόσμε· οὐδέν μοι πρόωρον οὐδὲ ὄψιμον ὃ σοὶ εὔκαιρον. πᾶν μοι καρπὸς ὃ φέρουσιν αἱ σαὶ ὧραι, ὦ φύσις· ἐκ σοῦ πάντα, ἐν σοὶ πάντα, εἰς σὲ πάντα. ἐκεῖνος μέν φησιν·

text: Look round at the courses of the stars, as if thou wert going along with them; and constantly consider the changes of the elements into one another; for such thoughts purge away the filth of the terrene life.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Adorn thyself with simplicity and with indifference towards the things which lie between virtue and vice. Love mankind. Follow God. The poet says that Law rules all. And it is enough to remember that law rules all.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Concepts and forms are born from our oversight of the individual and actual; nature, however, knows no such constructs, only an undefinable X, forever beyond our grasp.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: The artist's life is a battleground where two forces clash: the ordinary man's claim for happiness, and the ruthless creative passion that crushes all personal desires into dust.
author: Carl Jung

text: As a genius of construction man raises himself far above the bee in the following way: whereas the bee builds with wax that he gathers from nature, man builds with the far more delicate conceptual material which he first has to manufacture from himself.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: They are like optimistic tadpoles basking in a shallow puddle, amiably wriggling their tails, unaware that the next morning the puddle will have dried up and left them stranded.
author: Carl Jung
source: On a phallic dream he had as a young child. p. 14

text: Consider all that you've gone through, all that you've survived. And that the story of your life is done, your assignment complete. How many good things have you seen? How much pain and pleasure have you resisted? How many honors have you declined? How many unkind people have you been kind to?
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: All is in a man's hands, yet he lets it slip from cowardice. What men fear most is taking a new step, uttering a new word.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: Variant translation: "Taking a new step, uttering a new word, is what people fear most."

text: What happens to the individual contributes to the well-being of the world. The whole is damaged if you cut away anything from its continuity and coherence. When you complain, you are hacking and destroying.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Death hangs over thee: whilst yet thou livest, whilst thou mayest, be good.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: A horse at the end of the race...A dog when the hunt is over...A bee with its honey stored...And a human being after helping others. They don't make a fuss about it. They just go on to something else, as the vine looks forward to bearing fruit again in season. We should be like that. Acting almost unconsciously.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Search men's governing principles, and consider the wise, what they shun and what they cleave to.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Reverence the gods, and help men. Short is life.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: What does not make a man worse, does not make his life worse. It has no power to harm, without or within.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Nothing earthly succeeds by ignoring heaven, nothing heavenly by ignoring the earth.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Eros is a superhuman power, which, like nature, allows itself to be conquered and exploited as though impotent. But triumph over nature is dearly paid for. 'Eros is a mighty daemon,' as Diotima said to Socrates. We shall never get the better of him, or only to our own hurt.
author: Carl Jung
source: Two Essays on Analytical Psychology, CW 7 (1957). "On the Psychology of the Unconscious" P.32f

text: Mark how fleeting and paltry is the estate of man - yesterday in embryo, tomorrow a mummy or ashes. So for the hairsbreadth of time assigned to thee, live rationally, and part with life cheerfully, as drops the ripe olive, extolling the season that bore it and the tree that matured it.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: A man can lose neither the past nor the future, for how can one take from him that which is not his? Remember that each thing comes round again in its cycle, and that the longest-lived and shortest-lived man, when they come to die, lose one and the same thing.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Things have no hold on the soul. They have no access to it, cannot move or direct it. It is moved and directed by itself alone. It takes the things before it and interprets them as it sees fit.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: By a tranquil mind I mean nothing else than a mind well ordered.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: If you are distressed by anything external, the pain is not due to the thing itself, but to your estimate of it; and this you have the power to revoke at any moment.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: As surgeons keep their instruments always at hand, so should your thoughts be ready to understand things divine and human.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Nothing happens to anyone that he can't endure.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: Nothing happens to anybody which he is not fitted by nature to bear.

text: No state is sorrier than that of the man who pries into the secrets of the nether world, or is curious about what lies in his neighbor's heart.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: A man should be upright, not kept upright.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Do your work with diligence, energy, and patience. Keep your spirit undamaged, as if you might have to give it back at any moment. Find fulfillment in the present, without fear or expectation, and your life will be happy.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: But if we judge only those things which are in our power to be good or bad, there remains no reason either for finding fault with God or standing in a hostile attitude to man.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The meeting of two personalities is like the contact of two chemical substances: if there is any reaction, both are transformed.
author: Carl Jung
source_section: Modern Man in Search of a Soul (1933)

text: No psychic value can disappear without being replaced by another of equivalent intensity.
author: Carl Jung
source_section: Modern Man in Search of a Soul (1933)

text: The meaning and design of a problem seem not to lie in its solution, but in our working at it incessantly.
author: Carl Jung
source_section: Modern Man in Search of a Soul (1933)

text: The erotic instinct blooms only when the spirit and instinct are in true harmony. Too much of the animal disfigures the civilized human being; too much culture makes a sick animal.
author: Carl Jung

text: If anyone can convince me that I do not think or act rightly, I will gladly change; for I seek truth, by which no one is ever harmed. But he is harmed who abides in deception and ignorance.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: Variant translation: If someone is able to show me that what I think or do is not right, I will happily change, for I seek the truth, by which no one ever was truly harmed. Harmed is the person who continues in his self-deception and ignorance.

text: Pass by us, and forgive us our happiness.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: The whole nature of man presupposes woman, both physically and spiritually.
author: Carl Jung
source: " Two Essays in Analytical Psychology " In CW 7: P. 188 (1967)

text: Give thyself time to learn something new and good, and cease to be whirled around.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: How many together with whom I came into the world are already gone out of it.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Just as the sand-dunes, heaped one upon another, hide each the first, so in life the former deeds are quickly hidden by those that follow after.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: If mind is common, then reason and law are common, making us citizens of one constitution, and the Universe a kind of Commonwealth.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Art thou angry with him whose arm-pits stink? art thou angry with him whose mouth smells foul? What good will this anger do thee?
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Every step closer to my soul excites the scornful laughter of my devils, those cowardly ear-whisperers and poison-mixers.
author: Carl Jung

text: Observe always that everything is the result of a change, and get used to thinking that there is nothing Nature loves so well as to change existing forms and to make new ones like them.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Ideas dismissed as 'not true' because they lack empirical proof are, I would say, merely 'not true enough.' For these are conceptions that have accompanied human life from prehistoric times, breaking through into consciousness at any provocation.
author: Carl Jung

text: As far as we can discern, the sole purpose of human existence is to kindle a light in the darkness of mere being.
author: Carl Jung

text: Be not unwilling in what thou doest, neither selfish nor unadvised nor obstinate; let not over-refinement deck out thy thought; be not wordy nor a busybody.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Whatever is in any way beautiful hath its source of beauty in itself, and is complete in itself; praise forms no part of it. So it is none the worse nor the better for being praised.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: Variant: That which is really beautiful has no need of anything. (trans. George Long )

text: Waste not the remnant of thy life in those imaginations touching other folk, whereby thou contributest not to the common weal.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: No one can flatter himself that he is immune to the spirit of his own epoch, or even that he possesses a full understanding of it. Each of us, a particle of the general mass, is attached to, colored by, or undermined by the spirit which pervades it. Freedom stretches only as far as the limits of our consciousness.
author: Carl Jung

text: Be straightforward. Look at things like a man, like a human being, like a citizen, like a mortal.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: To change thy mind upon occasion, and to follow him that is able to rectify thee, is equally ingenuous as to find out at the first what is right and just without help.
