For of thee nothing is required that is beyond the extent of thine own deliberation and judgment, and of thine own understanding.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: It is ignoble for the face to obey the mind's command, yet for the mind itself to lack self-mastery.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: One will rarely err if extreme actions be ascribed to vanity, ordinary actions to habit, and mean actions to fear.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Time is a sort of river of passing events, and strong is its current; no sooner is a thing brought to sight than it is swept by and another takes its place, and this too will be swept away.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Remember this— that there is a proper dignity and proportion to be observed in the performance of every act of life.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: What is not good for the swarm is not good for the bee.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: It is crazy to want what is impossible. And impossible for the wicked not to do so.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: To seek what is impossible is madness: and it is impossible that the bad should not do something of this kind.

text: That which had grown from the earth, to the earth, But that which has sprung from heavenly seed, Back to the heavenly realms returns.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: All my thoughts circle around God like the planets around the sun, and are as irresistibly attracted by Him.
author: Carl Jung
source: Sources: David John Tacey (2007). How to read Jung . W.W. Norton & Co, p. 35; Charles Bartruff Hanna (1967). The Face of the Deep: The Religious Ideas of C.G. Jung. “The” Westminster Press, p. 18; Nándor Fodor (1971). Freud, Jung, and occultism. University Books. p. 12; Wayne G. Rollins (1983). Jung and the Bible. p. 123

text: Don't let yourself forget how many doctors have died, after furrowing their brows over how many deathbeds. How many astrologers, after pompous forecasts about others' ends. How many philosophers, after endless disquisitions on death and immortality. How many warriors, after inflicting thousands of casualties themselves. How many tyrants, after abusing the power of life and death atrociously, as if they were themselves immortal.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: In all chaos there is cosmos, in all disorder a secret order.
author: Carl Jung
source: Translation: In all chaos there is a cosmos, in all disorder a secret order.
source_section: The Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious (1934)

text: It is royal to do good and be abused.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The wise man who is not heeded is counted a fool, and the fool who proclaims the general folly first and loudest passes for a prophet and Führer.
author: Carl Jung

text: I am an orphan, alone; nevertheless I am found everywhere. I am one, but opposed to myself. I am youth and old man at one and the same time. I have known neither father nor mother, because I have had to be fetched out of the deep like a fish, or fell like a white stone from heaven. In woods and mountains I roam, but I am hidden in the innermost soul of man. I am mortal for everyone, yet I am not touched by the cycle of aeons.
author: Carl Jung

text: The intelligence of the universe is social.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Awaken your mind from its dreams. When you realize your troubles were but dreams, view worldly things with the same detachment as you would the visions of sleep.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Our destiny exercises its influence over us even when, as yet, we have not learned its nature: it is our future that lays down the law of our today.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Some people do favors expecting repayment, others acknowledge the debt, but the truly generous are like a vine that produces grapes without looking for anything in return.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: A man makes no noise over a good deed, but passes on to another as a vine to bear grapes again in season.

text: Among all my patients in the second half of life, not one problem ultimately stemmed from anything other than the lack of a religious outlook. They fell ill having lost what living religions offer, and none truly healed without regaining it.
author: Carl Jung
source: Chap. 11 ( Psychotherapists or the Clergy ), p. 229
source_section: Modern Man in Search of a Soul (1933)

text: The impediment to action advances action. What stands in the way becomes the way.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: It is astounding that man, the instigator and planner of the future, must make himself such a negligible quantity.
author: Carl Jung

text: Your vision will become clear only when you can look into your own heart. Without, everything seems discordant; only within does it coalesce into unity. Who looks outside dreams; who looks inside awakes.
author: Carl Jung

text: The unconscious is not just evil by nature, it is also the source of the highest good: not only dark but also light, not only bestial but superhuman, spiritual, and divine.
author: Carl Jung

text: One must have a good memory to be able to keep the promises one makes.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Those who have forgotten where the road leads are at odds with what is all around them, and find alien what they meet with every day.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: If one does not understand a person, one tends to regard him as a fool.
author: Carl Jung
source: Mysterium Coniunctionis , from The Collected Works of C. G. Jung (1966)

text: The span we live is small—small as the corner of the earth in which we live it. Small as even the greatest renown, passed from mouth to mouth by short-lived stick figures, ignorant alike of themselves and those long dead.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Primitive superstition lies just below the surface of even the most tough-minded individuals, and it is precisely those who most fight against it who are the first to succumb to its suggestive effects.
author: Carl Jung

text: If you are troubled by external circumstances, it is not the circumstances that trouble you, but your own perception of them, and they are in your power to change at any time.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: All that happens is as usual and familiar as the rose in spring and the crop in summer.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The woman who fights against her father still has the possibility of leading an instinctive, feminine existence, because she rejects only what is alien to her. But when she fights against the mother she may, at the risk of injury to her instincts, attain to greater consciousness, because in repudiating the mother she repudiates all that is obscure, instinctive, ambiguous, and unconscious in her own nature.
author: Carl Jung
source: "Psychological Aspects of the Mother Archetype" (1939). In CW 9, Part I: The Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious. P. 186

text: The things you think about determine the quality of your mind. Your soul takes on the color of your thoughts.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: The soul becomes dyed with the colour of its thoughts.

text: What is divine is full of Providence. Even chance is not divorced from nature, from the inweaving and enfolding of things governed by Providence. Everything proceeds from it.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: All that is from the gods is full of Providence.

text: You see how few things you have to do to live a satisfying and reverent life? If you can manage this, that's all even the gods can ask of you.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: Thou seest how few be the things, the which if a man has at his command his life flows gently on and is divine.

text: Metaphysical assertions, however, are statements of the psyche, and are therefore psychological. ... Whenever the Westerner hears the word “psychological,” it always sounds to him like “only psychological.”
author: Carl Jung

text: Nothing has such power to broaden the mind as the ability to investigate systematically and truly all that comes under thy observation in life.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Think not so much of what thou hast not as of what thou hast: but of the things which thou hast, select the best, and then reflect how eagerly they would have been sought, if thou hadst them not. At the same time, however, take care that thou dost not, through being so pleased with them, accustom thyself to overvalue them, so as to be disturbed if ever thou shouldst not have them.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: Variant Translation: Let not thy mind run on what thou lackest as much as on what thou hast already.

text: At dawn, when you have trouble getting out of bed, tell yourself: ‘I have to go to work – as a human being. What do I have to complain of, if I’m going to do what I was born for – the things I was brought into the world to do? Or is this what I was created for? To huddle under the blankets and stay warm?’
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: At dawn of day, when you dislike being called, have this thought ready: "I am called to man's labour; why then do I make a difficulty if I am going out to do what I was born to do and what I was brought into the world for?(Farquharson translation)

text: There are two sorts of mind: one that matters, and one that doesn't matter.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Madness is not to be despised and not to be feared, but instead you should give it life. If you want to find paths, you should also not spurn madness, since it makes up such a great part of your nature. Madness is a special form of the spirit and clings to all teachings and philosophies, but even more to daily life, since life itself is full of craziness and at bottom utterly illogical. Man strives toward reason only so that he can make rules for himself. Life itself has no rules. That is its mystery and its unknown law. What you call knowledge is an attempt to impose something comprehensible on life.
author: Carl Jung

text: Seldom, or perhaps never, does a marriage develop into an individual relationship smoothly and without crises; there is no coming to consciousness without pain.
author: Carl Jung

text: The healthy man does not torture others—generally it is the tortured who turn into torturers.
author: Carl Jung

text: Even if the whole world were to fall to pieces, the unity of the psyche would never be shattered. And the wider and more numerous the fissures on the surface, the more the unity is strengthened in the depths.
author: Carl Jung

text: Since it is not known where my center is, it won't be easy to find out where or when I have thus far been 'eccentric.'
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: It's unfortunate that this has happened. No. It's fortunate that this has happened and I've remained unharmed by it—not shattered by the present or frightened of the future. It could have happened to anyone. But not everyone could have remained unharmed by it.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Naturally, every age thinks that all ages before it were prejudiced, and today we think this more than ever and are just as wrong as all previous ages that thought so. How often have we not seen the truth condemned! It is sad but unfortunately true that man learns nothing from history.
author: Carl Jung

text: Always take the short cut; and that is the rational one. Therefore say and do everything according to soundest reason.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: As for life, it is a battle and a sojourning in a strange land; but the fame that comes after is oblivion.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Where any work can be done conformably to the reason which is common to gods and men, there we have nothing to fear.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Though thou be destined to live three thousand years and as many myriads besides, yet remember that no man loseth other life than that which he liveth, nor liveth other than that which he loseth.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Direct thy attention to what is said. Let thy understanding enter into the things that are doing and the things which do them.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Every father is given the opportunity to corrupt his daughter's nature. What has been spoiled by the father can only be made good by a father, just as what has been spoiled by the mother can only be repaired by a mother.
author: Carl Jung

text: Another may be more expert in casting his opponent; but he is not more social, nor more modest, nor better disciplined to meet all that happens, nor more considerate with respect to the faults of his neighbors.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Men seek retreats in the country, by the sea, or in the mountains. But it is in your power to retire into yourself, for nowhere does a man find more quiet or freedom from trouble than in his own soul.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: Variant translation: Nowhere can man find a quieter or more untroubled retreat than in his own soul.

text: The man who has an elevated mind and takes a view of all time and of all substance, cannot think that human life is anything great. Such a man will think that death also is no evil.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Aging people should know that their lives are not mounting and unfolding but that an inexorable inner process forces the contraction of life. For a young person it is almost a sin — and certainly a danger — to be too much occupied with himself; but for the aging person it is a duty and a necessity to give serious attention to himself.
author: Carl Jung
source_section: Modern Man in Search of a Soul (1933)

text: People try to get away from it all—to the country, to the beach, to the mountains. You always wish that you could too. Which is idiotic: you can get away from it anytime you like. By going within. Nowhere you can go is more peaceful—more free of interruptions—than your own soul.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: What does man actually know about himself? Is he, indeed, ever able to perceive himself completely, as if laid out in a lighted display case? Does nature not conceal most things from him — even concerning his own body — in order to confine and lock him within a proud, deceptive consciousness, aloof from the coils of the bowels, the rapid flow of the blood stream, and the intricate quivering of the fibers! She threw away the key.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: The lot assigned to every man is suited to him, and suits him to itself.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: When troubled by hard occurrences, return to yourself as soon as possible. Do not remain out of tune longer than necessary, for this practice will enable you to maintain your harmony.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Thou art a little soul bearing about a corpse.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: He was a man who looked at what ought to be done, not to the reputation which is got by a man's acts.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: If you want to be respected by others the great thing is to respect yourself. Only by that, only by self-respect will you compel others to respect you.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: In science I missed the factor of meaning; and in religion, that of empiricism.
author: Carl Jung

text: Death,—a stopping of impressions through the senses, and of the pulling of the cords of motion, and of the ways of thought, and of service to the flesh.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The greatest and most important problems of life are all in a certain sense insoluble. They can never be solved, but only outgrown.
author: Carl Jung
source: The Secret of the Golden Flower, ibid.

text: Never esteem anything as of advantage to you that will make you break your word or lose your self-respect.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Is any man afraid of change? Why what can take place without change?
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The only thing that isn't worthless: to live this life out truthfully and rightly. And be patient with those who don't.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Man needs difficulties; they are necessary for health.
author: Carl Jung

text: Although the most acute judges of the witches and even the witches themselves, were convinced of the guilt of witchery, the guilt nevertheless was non-existent. It is thus with all guilt.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: As translated in The Portable Nietzsche (1954) by Walter Kaufmann , p. 96

text: The God wants my life. He wants to go with me, sit at the table with me, work with me. Above all, he wants to be ever-present.
author: Carl Jung

text: Whatever this is that I am, it is flesh and a little spirit and an intelligence.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: This that I am, whatever it be, is mere flesh and a little breathe and the ruling Reason (Haines translation)

text: Deem not life a thing of consequence. For look at the yawning void of the future, and at that other limitless space, the past.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Constantly regard the universe as one living being, having one substance and one soul; and observe how all things have reference to one perception, the perception of this one living being; and how all things act with one movement; and how all things are the cooperating causes of all things which exist; observe too the continuous spinning of the thread and the contexture of the web.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: You could leave life right now. Let that determine what you do and say and think.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Beauty does not lie in things, but in the feeling we give to them.
author: Carl Jung

text: Not to feel exasperated, or defeated, or despondent because your days aren't packed with wise and moral actions. But to get back up when you fail, to celebrate behaving like a human--however imperfectly--and fully embrace the pursuit that you've embarked on.
author: Marcus Aurelius
source: Flinch not, neither give up nor despair, if the achieving of every act in accordance with right principle is not always continuous with thee.

text: How many, once lauded in song, are given over to the forgotten; and how many who sung their praises are clean gone long ago!
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: There is a limit to the time assigned you, and if you don't use it to free yourself it will be gone and never return.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Love the little trade which thou hast learned, and be content therewith.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: If there is anything that we wish to change in the child, we should first examine it and see whether it is not something that could better be changed in ourselves.
author: Carl Jung

text: For it all depends on how we look at things, and not on how they are in themselves. The least of things with a meaning is worth more in life than the greatest of things without it.
author: Carl Jung
source_section: Modern Man in Search of a Soul (1933)

text: The abyss of endless time that swallows it all. The emptiness of all those applauding hands. The people who praise us—how capricious they are, how arbitrary. And the tiny region in which it all takes place.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Does anything genuinely beautiful need supplementing? No more than justice does—or truth, or kindness, or humility. Are any of those improved by being praised? Or damaged by contempt? Is an emerald suddenly flawed if no one admires it? Or gold, or ivory, or purple? Lyres? Knives? Flowers? Bushes?
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Experiment forces Nature to answer questions devised by man, preventing her from revealing the fullness of her possibilities. To understand Nature's unrestricted wholeness, we need a method of inquiry that imposes the fewest possible conditions, allowing her to answer from her own abundance.
author: Carl Jung

text: The conscious mind allows itself to be trained like a parrot, but the unconscious does not — which is why St. Augustine thanked God for not making him responsible for his dreams.
author: Carl Jung

text: I do what is mine to do; the rest doesn't disturb me.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Without the play of fantasy, no creative work has ever come to birth. The debt we owe to imagination is incalculable.
author: Carl Jung
source_section: Psychological Types , or, The Psychology of Individuation (1921)

text: Not to display anger or other emotions. To be free of passion and yet full of love.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The seat of faith is not consciousness, but spontaneous religious experience, which brings the individual into immediate relation with God.
author: Carl Jung

text: The secret is that only that which can destroy itself is truly alive.
author: Carl Jung

text: Respect the faculty that forms thy judgments.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Whatever happens, happens as it should.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Just that you do the right thing. The rest doesn't matter. Cold or warm. Tired or well-rested. Despised or honored. Dying . . . or busy with other assignments.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: No matter what anyone says or does, my task is to be good.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The world is nothing but change. Our life is only perception.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Doth perfect beauty stand in need of praise at all? Nay; no more than law, no more than truth, no more than loving kindness, nor than modesty.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Each of us lives only now, this brief instant.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The best revenge is not to be like your enemy.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Not to live as if you had endless years ahead of you. Death overshadows you. While you're alive and able—be good.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Choose not to be harmed—and you won't feel harmed. Don't feel harmed—and you haven't been.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Concentrate every minute like a Roman—like a man—on doing what's in front of you with precise and genuine seriousness, tenderly, willingly, with justice. And on freeing yourself from all other distractions.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The mind is the ruler of the soul. It should remain unstirred by agitations of the flesh. Don't resist natural sensations, but don't let the mind judge them as 'good' or 'bad.'
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Trust no one in whom the desire to punish is strong
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
source: Variants of such a statement have also been attributed to Johann Wolfgang von Goethe and Friedrich Nietzsche , but the only definite source found for any is in Nietzsche's Thus Spoke Zarathustra : " Distrust all in whom the impulse to punish is powerful! "

text: What you are dominated by in your childhood is whatever your parents really love.
author: Carl Jung
source: Muriel Rukeyser “The Education of a Poet” (1976)

text: All ordinary expression may be explained causally, but creative expression which is the absolute contrary of ordinary expression, will be forever hidden from human knowledge.
author: Carl Jung

text: For we carry our fate with us — and it carries us.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Most of what we say and do is not essential. Eliminate it, and you'll have more time and tranquillity. Ask yourself at every moment, 'Is this necessary?'
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The little world of childhood is a model of the greater world. The more intensively the family stamps its character upon the child, the more it will tend to see its miniature world again in adult life.
author: Carl Jung

text: So remember this principle when something threatens to cause you pain: the thing itself was no misfortune at all; to endure it and prevail is great good fortune.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: When faced with the invisible and unknowable—God beyond understanding, immortality unproven—why do we demand evidence?
author: Carl Jung

text: Life has always seemed to me like a plant that lives on its rhizome . Its true life is invisible, hidden in the rhizome. The part that appears above ground lasts only a single summer. What we see is the blossom, which passes. The rhizome remains.
author: Carl Jung

text: All ages before ours believed in gods in some form or other. Only an unparalleled impoverishment in symbolism could enable us to rediscover the gods as psychic factors, which is to say, as archetypes of the unconscious. No doubt this discovery is hardly credible as yet.
author: Carl Jung

text: The longest-lived and the shortest-lived man, when they come to die, lose one and the same thing.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The dream is the small hidden door in the deepest and most intimate sanctum of the soul , which opens to that primeval cosmic night that was soul long before there was conscious ego and will be soul far beyond what a conscious ego could ever reach.
author: Carl Jung
source: The Meaning of Psychology for Modern Man (1934)

text: People will do anything, no matter how absurd, in order to avoid facing their own souls. Thus the soul has gradually been turned into a Nazareth from which nothing good can come.
author: Carl Jung

text: We should not pretend to understand the world only by the intellect; we apprehend it just as much by feeling. Therefore, the judgment of the intellect is, at best, only the half of truth, and must, if it be honest, also come to an understanding of its inadequacy.
author: Carl Jung
source: Variant translation: We should not pretend to understand the world only by the intellect. The judgement of the intellect is only part of the truth.
source_section: Psychological Types , or, The Psychology of Individuation (1921)

text: The woman is increasingly aware that love alone can give her full stature, just as the man begins to discern that spirit alone can endow his life with its highest meaning. Fundamentally, therefore, both seek a psychic relation to the other, because love needs the spirit, and the spirit love, for their fulfillment.
author: Carl Jung

text: Rarely does a creative person not pay a high price for the divine spark of their gifts; the human element is frequently bled for the creative.
author: Carl Jung

text: The great problems of life are always rooted in the primordial images of the collective unconscious, acting as balancing factors for the challenges life presents.
author: Carl Jung
source_section: Psychological Types , or, The Psychology of Individuation (1921)

text: We always require an outside point to apply the lever of criticism. Indeed, everything that irritates us about others can lead us to an understanding of ourselves.
author: Carl Jung

text: You can take away a man's gods, but only to give him others in return.
author: Carl Jung

text: No nation keeps its word. A nation is a big, blind worm, without honor or a word to keep.
author: Carl Jung
source: During an interview with H. R. Knickerbocker (1939), quoted in A Life of Jung (2002) by Ronald Hayman, p. 360

text: I must also have a dark side if I am to be whole.
author: Carl Jung

text: The world exists only as it is consciously reflected by a psyche; thus, consciousness is a precondition of being.
author: Carl Jung

text: The man who does not wish to belong to the mass needs only to cease taking himself easily; let him follow his conscience, which calls to him: “Be your self! All you are now doing, thinking, desiring, is not you yourself.”
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: “ Schopenhauer as educator,” § 3.1, R. Hollingdale , trans. (1983), p. 127

text: Men who sever contact with the unconscious and its symbolic meaning often fall sick, both psychologically and physically. The true purpose of religion is to keep humanity connected to this deeper reality, largely through myth.
author: Carl Jung
source: Morton Kelsey, Myth, History & Faith: The Mysteries of Christian Myth & Imagination (1974) Ch.VII

text: Every man has reminiscences he would tell only to friends; others, only to himself in secret. But there are still others he is even afraid to tell himself, and the more decent he is, the greater the number of such things in his mind.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: I am foremost of all the Trojan warriors to stave the day of bondage from off them; as for you, vultures shall devour you here.
author: Homer
source: XVI (tr. Samuel Butler); Hector to Patroclus.

text: To say that the activity of science and art helps humanity's progress is like saying the clumsy, obstructive splashing of oars in a boat moving downstream assists its progress. It only hinders it.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Error is the force that welds men together; truth is communicated only by deeds. Only deeds of truth, by introducing light into the conscience of each individual, can dissolve the cohesion of error and detach men from the mass.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: I know that my unity with all people cannot be destroyed by national boundaries and government orders.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: My Religion (1884), as translated in The Human Experience : Contemporary American and Soviet Fiction and Poetry (1989) by the Quaker US/USSR Committee

text: In former days, wars were limited; now, mighty populations are impelled on each other, each individual embittered and inflamed. When science and civilization sweep away everything that might mitigate their fury, a war can only end in the ruin of the vanquished and the scarcely less fatal exhaustion of the conquerors. Democracy is more vindictive than Cabinets. The wars of peoples will be more terrible than those of kings.
author: Winston Churchill
source: House of Commons, 13 May 1901, Hansard vol. 93 col. 1572.

text: The realization of desires often yields no more than a grain of sand from the mountain of expected happiness. Men mistake happiness for the fulfillment of desires, only to find a 'desire for desires' springing up in their hearts—a perpetual longing.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: An infected Russia, a plague-bearing Russia; a Russia of armed hordes not only smiting with bayonet and with cannon, but accompanied and preceded by swarms of typhus-bearing vermin which slew the bodies of men, and political doctrines which destroyed the health and even the souls of nations.
author: Winston Churchill
source: The Aftermath , by Winston Churchill (1929), p. 274

text: To live in official spheres, dealing only with the reflection of life, is to shrink from life itself. It is like calmly crossing a precipice by a bridge, only to discover it broken, revealing the chasm below. That chasm is life itself; the bridge, the artificial life one has lived.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Oft of one wide expanse had I been told That deep-browed Homer ruled as his demesne; Yet never did I breathe its pure serene Till I heard Chapman speak out loud and bold: Then felt I like some watcher of the skies When a new planet swims into his ken.
author: Homer
source: John Keats , On First Looking into Chapman's Homer (1817)

text: History and poetry, justice and good sense, alike demand that this race, gifted, virtuous, and brave, which has lived so long and has endured so much, should not, in view of her passionate desire, be left out of the family of nations, and should not be lost forever among the indiscriminated multitudes of men.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Speech in Celtic Park Football Ground in Belfast (8 February 1912), quoted in Never Give In! The Best of Winston Churchill's Speeches (2003), pp. 48-49

text: No hour of life is lost that is spent in the saddle.
author: Winston Churchill
source: My early life, 1874–1904 (1930), Churchill, Winston S., p. 45 (1996 Touchstone Edition), ISBN 0684823454
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: It is better to be making the news than taking it; to be an actor rather than a critic.
author: Winston Churchill
source: The Story of the Malakand Field Force: An Episode of Frontier War (1898), Chapter VIII

text: To recognize this clearly is enough to drive a man out of his senses or to make him shoot himself.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter V , Contradiction Between our Life and our Christian Conscience
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: It is not merely the waste of earning-power or the dispersal of a few poor sticks of furniture; it is the stamina, the virtue, safety, and honour of the British race that are being squandered.
author: Winston Churchill

text: The strongest of all warriors are these two — Time and Patience.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: We are not ostriches, and cannot believe that if we refuse to look at what we do not wish to see, it will not exist.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Mr. Gladstone read Homer for fun, which I thought served him right.
author: Homer

text: Every violent reform deserves censure, for it quite fails to remedy evil while men remain what they are, and also because wisdom needs no violence.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: One can insult an honest man or an honest woman, but to tell a thief that he is a thief is merely to state a fact.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: To be good and lead a good life means to give to others more than one takes from them.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Science has adapted itself entirely to the wealthy classes and accordingly has set itself to heal those who can afford everything, and it prescribes the same methods for those who have nothing to spare.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: There is only one enduring happiness in life—to live for others.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Revolutionaries seek to destroy and replace bad government. A Christian, knowing nothing of its good or ill, simply refuses to support it.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter IX , The Acceptance of the Christian Conception of Life will Emancipate Men from the Miseries of our Pagan Life
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: Nothing in life is so exhilarating as to be shot at without result.
author: Winston Churchill
source: The Story of the Malakand Field Force: An Episode of Frontier War (1898), Chapter X

text: Hegelianism, like pseudo-Christianity, fell not by refutation, but by becoming irrelevant to the learned world.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: As long as you are generous and true, and also fierce, you cannot hurt the world or even seriously distress her.
author: Winston Churchill
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: Engage people with what they expect; it is what they are able to discern and confirms their projections. It settles them into predictable patterns of response, occupying their minds while you wait for the extraordinary moment — that which they cannot anticipate.
author: Sun Tzu

text: Politics are almost as exciting as war , and – quite as dangerous ... [I]n war, you can only be killed once. But in politics many times.
author: Winston Churchill
source: From a conversational exchange with Harold Begbie, as cited in Master Workers , Begbie, Methuen & Co. (1906), p. 177.

text: All violence consists in some people forcing others, under threat of suffering or death , to do what they do not want to do.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: The Law of Love and the Law of Violence (1908)

text: Progress consists only in the greater clarification of answers to the basic questions of life. The truth is obtained like gold, not by letting it grow bigger, but by washing off from it everything that isn't gold.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Tolstoy's Diaries (1985) edited and translated by R. F. Christian. London: Athlone Press, Vol 2, p. 512

text: You will die — and it will all be over. You will die and find out everything — or cease asking.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Violence breeds violence; deliverance lies in not taking part. Those who neither struggle against violence nor participate in it can no more be enslaved than water can be cut.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: The Meaning of the Russian Revolution (1906)

text: Do men really not have to answer for such things?
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: The well-to-do, in discussing workers' improvements, only ever envision changes that preserve the factory system and their own comforts.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter V: Why Learned Economists Assert What Is False

text: The error lies in learned jurists deceiving themselves and others, asserting that government is not what it truly is—one set of men banded to oppress another—but merely the representation of citizens in their collective capacity.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter VI , Attitude of Men of the Present Day to War
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: Like chasing a quinine pill around a cow pasture.
author: Winston Churchill
source: On playing golf : as cited in The quote verifier: who said what, where, and when (2006), Keyes, Macmillan, p. 27 ISBN 0312340044

text: He's cultivated, one sees, simply to be able to despise culture, as they despise everything but animal pleasures.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Vronky and Anna discussing the visiting Prince, Part 4, Chapter 3

text: One can only live while one is intoxicated with life; as soon as one is sober it is impossible not to see that it is all a mere fraud and a stupid fraud!
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: The wisdom of hereditary monarchy lies in placing the supreme leadership of the State beyond the reach of private ambition and above the shocks and changes of party strife.
author: Winston Churchill

text: But when to examine every part he came, Nature and Homer were, he found, the same.
author: Homer
source: Alexander Pope , An Essay on Criticism (1711), lines 134–135

text: The slaves of our times are not merely factory hands, but agricultural laborers toiling for others or for endless debt, and all those who perform unnatural, disliked duties their whole lives long.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: I think a curse should rest on me — because I love this war. I know it's smashing and shattering the lives of thousands every moment — and yet — I can't help it — I enjoy every second of it.
author: Winston Churchill

text: I am indignant whenever good Homer nods.
author: Homer
source: I'm aggrieved when sometimes even excellent Homer nods.

text: His gods are perhaps at once absurd and entertaining.
author: Homer

text: Universal hypocrisy has so permeated modern society that nothing of its kind can rouse indignation, for hypocrisy, in Greek, means 'acting,' and playing a part is always possible.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter XII , Conclusion—Repent Ye, for the Kingdom of Heaven is at Hand
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: Lenin was sent into Russia by the Germans in the same way that you might send a phial containing a culture of typhoid or cholera to be poured into the water supply of a great city, and it worked with amazing accuracy.
author: Winston Churchill
source: On Vladimir Ilyich Lenin , in the House of Commons, November 5, 1919 as cited in Churchill by Himself (2008), Ed. Langworth, PublicAffairs, p. 355 ISBN 1586486381

text: Every heart and eye sees that working men pass their lives in want and useless toil, while others, who do not work, enjoy the fruits of their labor—that they are slaves, and this ought not to exist.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: "To the Working People," Complete Works , trans. Leo Wiener, Vol 24, p. 129 (1905)

text: In historical events great men — so-called — are but labels serving to give a name to the event, and like labels they have the least possible connection with the event itself. Every action of theirs, that seems to them an act of their own free will, is in an historical sense not free at all, but in bondage to the whole course of previous history, and predestined from all eternity .
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Quite often a man goes on for years imagining that the religious teaching that had been imparted to him since childhood is still intact, while all the time there is not a trace of it left in him.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: The whole life of the upper classes is a constant inconsistency. The more delicate a man's conscience is, the more painful this contradiction is to him.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter V , Contradiction Between our Life and our Christian Conscience
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: Mr. Gladstone read Homer for fun, which I thought served him right.
author: Winston Churchill
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: If one has no vanity in this life of ours, there is no sufficient reason for living .
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Ch. 23. This is not, as it is often quoted, a stand-alone Tolstoy epigram, but part of the narration by the novella's jealousy-ridden protagonist Pozdnyshev.

text: The only thing that we know is that we know nothing — and that is the highest flight of human wisdom.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Pure and complete sorrow is as impossible as pure and complete joy.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: She shone for me like the Evening Star. I loved her dearly — but at a distance.
author: Winston Churchill
source: On his mother, Lady Randolph Churchill, Chapter 1 (Childhood).
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: Just as water retains no constant shape, so in warfare there are no constant conditions.
author: Sun Tzu

text: O fortunate youth, who has found Homer as the herald of your valor!
author: Homer
source: O happy youth! in having found a Homer to celebrate thy virtues!

text: Never, never, never believe any war will be smooth and easy, or that anyone who embarks on the strange voyage can measure the tides and hurricanes he will encounter. The statesman who yields to war fever must realise that once the signal is given, he is no longer the master of policy but the slave of unforeseeable and uncontrollable events. Always remember, however sure you are that you could easily win, that there would not be a war if the other man did not think he also had a chance.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Chapter 18 (With Buller To The Cape), p. 246
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: Homer has given to military life a charm which few men can resist, a splendor that envelopes carnage in a cloud of glory, dazzling eyes, stealing our natural sensibilities for the artificial, debasing men to brutes while exalting them to gods, and obliterating the moral duties of life.
author: Homer
source: Joel Barlow , Advice to the Privileged Orders in the Several States of Europe, Resulting from the Necessity and Propriety of a General Revolution in the Principle of Government. Part I. (1792), pp. 76-77

text: I cannot recall those years without horror, loathing, and heart-rending pain. I killed people in war, challenged men to duels with the purpose of killing them, and lost at cards; I squandered the fruits of the peasants' toil and then had them executed; I was a fornicator and a cheat. Lying, stealing, promiscuity of every kind, drunkenness, violence, murder — there was not a crime I did not commit. Thus I lived for ten years.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: We cannot persuade ourselves that soldiers and police exist merely for defense or traffic, nor that men willingly die of hunger or toil endlessly in stifling conditions for our pleasure. One would imagine it impossible to deny what is so obvious. Yet it is denied.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter XII , Conclusion—Repent Ye, for the Kingdom of Heaven is at Hand
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: I pass with relief from the tossing sea of Cause and Theory to the firm ground of Result and Fact .
author: Winston Churchill
source: The Story of the Malakand Field Force: An Episode of Frontier War (1898), Chapter III

text: My reason will still not understand why I pray, but I shall still pray, and my life, my whole life, independently of anything that may happen to me, is every moment of it no longer meaningless as it was before, but has an unquestionable meaning of goodness with which I have the power to invest it.'
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Ivan Ilych's life had been most simple and most ordinary and therefore most terrible.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: The only absolute knowledge attainable by man is that life is meaningless.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: I decline utterly to be impartial as between the fire brigade and the fire.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Speech in the House of Commons, July 7, 1926 "Emergency Services" , responding to criticism that he edited the British Gazette in a biased manner during the General Strike , as cited in The Yale Book of Quotations (2006), ed. Fred R. Shapiro, Yale University Press, p. 152 ISBN 0300107986

text: There can be only one permanent revolution — a moral one; the regeneration of the inner man. How is this revolution to take place? Nobody knows how it will take place in humanity, but every man feels it clearly in himself. And yet in our world everybody thinks of changing humanity, and nobody thinks of changing himself.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: "Three Methods Of Reform" in Pamphlets : Translated from the Russian (1900) as translated by Aylmer Maude, p. 29

text: Seize the moments of happiness, love and be loved! That is the only reality in the world, all else is folly.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: In all history, no war was hatched save by governments, independent of the people's interests, to whom war is always pernicious. Governments assure people of danger, demanding slavish obedience, and justify their violence by claiming worse would befall without them.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Christianity and Patriotism (1895), as translated in The Novels and Other Works of Lyof N. Tolstoï , Vol. 20, p. 44

text: Read Homer once, and you can read no more, For all things else will seem so dull and poor, You'l wish 't unread; but oft upon him look, And you will hardly need another book.
author: Homer
source: John Sheffield , An Essay upon Poetry (1682)

text: At the approach of danger, two voices speak in the heart: one reasonably urges consideration and avoidance; the other, even more reasonably, suggests it's too painful to think of, and better to turn to what is pleasant. In solitude, man yields to the first; in society, to the second.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Life's answer to all complex and insoluble questions is to live for the needs of the day, to become oblivious. Since oblivion in dreams was impossible, one had to become oblivious in the dreams of life.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Where my reason, imagination or interest were not engaged, I would not or I could not learn.
author: Winston Churchill
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: Men of modern times strive to stupefy themselves, for to live in opposition to one's reason is the most intolerable condition. All men of the modern world exist in a state of continual antagonism between their conscience and their way of life, most strikingly in the contradiction of being at once a Christian and a gladiator.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter V , Contradiction Between our Life and our Christian Conscience
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: Prolonged education, indispensable to society's progress, is not natural to mankind; it cuts against the grain. A real love of learning comes to those who are worthy, opening the 'magic casements' of the mind.
author: Winston Churchill
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: The truth is that the State is a conspiracy designed not only to exploit, but above all to corrupt its citizens ... Henceforth, I shall never serve any government anywhere.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: As quoted in Tolstoy (1988) by A. N. Wilson , p. 146

text: Men grow tired of sleep, love, singing and dancing, sooner than of war.
author: Homer
source: A misquotation of: Πάντων μὲν κόρος ἐστὶ καὶ ὕπνου καὶ φιλότητος μολπῆς τε γλυκερῆς καὶ ἀμύμονος ὀρχηθμοῖο, τῶν πέρ τις καὶ μᾶλλον ἐέλδεται ἐξ ἔρον εἷναι ἢ πολέμου· Τρῶες δὲ μάχης ἀκόρητοι ἔασιν. Men get Their fill of all things, of sleep and love, sweet song And flawless dancing, and most men like these things Much better than war. Only Trojans are always Thirsty for blood! Iliad , XIII, 636–639 (tr. Ennis Rees) The misquotation implies that an overweening love of war was the norm, whereas the real quote decries the Trojans as inhumane for keeping the war going.

text: Jellicoe was the only man on either side who could lose the war in an afternoon.
author: Winston Churchill
source: The World Crisis, 1916-1918 Part I : Chapter V (Jutland: The Preliminaries), Churchill, Butterworth (1927), pp. 112

text: The most difficult subjects can be explained to the most slow-witted man if he has not formed any idea of them already; but the simplest thing cannot be made clear to the most intelligent man if he is firmly persuaded that he knows already, without a shadow of doubt , what is laid before him.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter III , Christianity Misunderstood by Believers
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: Each time I read The Iliad, I am convinced I have been face to face with the meaning of existence. No other work gives this insight so powerfully, so uncontaminated by evasion or subterfuge. Its purity, simplicity, and impact reveal life and expose it to irrevocable judgment.
author: Homer

text: Cultured people are merely the glittering scum which floats upon the deep river of production.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Quoted in Randolph Churchill's diary entry (24 August 1929), quoted in Martin Gilbert, The Churchill Documents, Volume 12: The Wilderness Years, 1929–1935 (1981; 2012), p. 55

text: Is this the end? Is it to be merely a chapter in a cruel and senseless story? Will a new generation in their turn be immolated to square the black accounts of the Teuton and Gaul? Will our children bleed and gasp again in devastated lands? Or will there spring from the very fires of conflict that reconciliation of the three giant combatants, which would unite their genius and secure to each in safety and freedom a share in rebuilding the glory of Europe.
author: Winston Churchill
source: The World Crisis, 1916-1918 Part II : Chapter XXIII (Victory), Churchill, Butterworth (1927), p. 544

text: Patriotism once welded mankind into states, but that work is done. Why maintain exclusive devotion to one's own state when it produces terrible evils for all nations? The very patriotism that once united now destroys. When national patriotisms stand opposed, they no longer unite, but disunite.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: A blind man; he dwells on the rocky Chios ; and his songs are the first among men.
author: Homer
source: Homeric "Hymn to Delian Apollo", cited by Thucydides , iii.104, as quoted in Arnold H. L. Heeren 's Reflections on the Politics of Ancient Greece , trans. George Bancroft (1824), footnote on p. 119

text: As learned commentators view In Homer more than Homer knew.
author: Homer

text: I suggest that Homer's Iliad and Tacitus' Germania should be given high priority among the hundred most dangerous books ever written. Who will deny that they raise most unholy passions in the human mind? The Iliad not only created the model of all those Achilleses and Agamemmnons who have troubled the world ever since, but bad historians have learnt from Homer to attribute silly causes to earnest wars.
author: Homer
source: Arnaldo Momigliano , 'Some Observations on Causes of War in Ancient Historiography', Acta Congressus Madvigiant , Proceedings of the Second International Congress of Classical Studies 1954, vol. I (1958), pp. 199-211, quoted in A. D. Momigliano, Studies in Historiography (1966; 1969), pp. 112-113

text: Ivan Ilych saw that he was dying, and he was in continual despair. In the depth of his heart he knew he was dying, but not only was he not accustomed to the thought, he simply did not and could not grasp it. The syllogism he had learnt from Kiesewetter's Logic: "Caius is a man, men are mortal, therefore Caius is mortal," had always seemed to him correct as applied to Caius, but certainly not as applied to himself. That Caius — man in the abstract — was mortal, was perfectly correct, but he was not Caius, not an abstract man, but a creature quite, quite separate from all others.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: The poems of Homer and his contemporaries were the delight of infant Greece; they were the elements of that social system which is the column upon which all succeeding civilization has reposed. Homer embodied the ideal perfection of his age in human character; nor can we doubt that those who read his verses were awakened to an ambition of becoming like to Achilles, Hector, and Ulysses: the truth and beauty of friendship, patriotism, and persevering devotion to an object, were unveiled to the depths in these immortal creations.
author: Homer
source: Percy Bysshe Shelley , A Defence of Poetry (1821)

text: We are at the cross-ways. If we stand on the old happy-go-lucky way, the richer classes ever growing in wealth and in number, and ever declining in responsibility, the very poor remaining plunged or plunging even deeper into helpless, hopeless misery, then there is nothing before us but savage strife between class and class, with increasing disorganization and destruction of human strength and virtue—nothing but that dual degeneration which comes from the simultaneous waste of extreme wealth and of extreme want.
author: Winston Churchill

text: A man can live and be healthy without killing animals for food; therefore, if he eats meat, he participates in taking animal life merely for the sake of his appetite. And to act so is immoral.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Writings on Civil Disobedience and Nonviolence (1886)

text: Science is meaningless because it gives no answer to our question, the only question important for us: 'what shall we do and how shall we live
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Quoted by Max Weber in his lecture "Science as a Vocation"; in Lynda Walsh (2013), Scientists as Prophets: A Rhetorical Genealogy (2013), Oxford University Press, p. 90

text: It is not sufficient to observe our mysteries in seclusion; we must act! For the dissemination of pure truth and the triumph of virtue, we must cleanse men from prejudice, diffuse enlightened principles, and boldly overcome superstition, infidelity, and folly.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: He stepped down, trying not to look long at her, as if she were the sun, yet he saw her, like the sun, even without looking.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: [The] truth is incontrovertible. Panic may resent it, ignorance may deride it, malice may distort it, but there it is.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Speech in the House of Commons, May 17, 1916 "Royal Assent" .

text: The hero of my tale, whom I love with all the power of my soul, whom I have tried to portray in all his beauty, who has been, is, and will be beautiful, is Truth.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Taxes are an evil—a necessary evil, but still an evil, and the fewer of them we have the better.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Churchill By Himself: The Definitive Collections of Quotations , ed. Richard Langworth, 2008, p. 424, (1907, 12 February)

text: The truth is that Gandhi-ism and all it stands for will, sooner or later, have to be grappled with, and finally crushed. It is no use trying to satisfy a tiger by feeding him with cat's-meat. The sooner this is realised, the less trouble and misfortune will there be for all concerned.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Speech in Cannon Street Hotel, London (12 December 1930) at the first public meeting of the Indian Empire Society , quoted in Martin Gilbert, Prophet of Truth: Winston S. Churchill, 1922–1939 (1976), p. 377

text: When all was over, Torture and Cannibalism were the only two expedients that the civilized, scientific, Christian States had been able to deny themselves: and these were of doubtful utility.
author: Winston Churchill
source: The World Crisis, 1911–1914 : Chapter I (The Vials of Wrath), Churchill, Butterworth (1923), pp. 10-11

text: Every word and action of his was the manifestation of an activity unknown to him, which was his life.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: About Platon Karataev in Bk. XII, ch. 13

text: In peace, prepare for war. In war, prepare for peace.
author: Sun Tzu
source: Sometimes erroneously prepended to the opening line "The art of war is of vital importance to the State", but appears to be a variation of the Roman motto "Si vis pacem, para bellum". It is not clear who first misattributed this phrase to Sun Tzu. The earliest appearance of the phrase in Google Books is 1920, when it appeared in a pharmaceutical journal, but no attribution was given then.

text: Our author's work is a wild paradise, where, if we cannot see all the beauties so distinctly as in an ordered garden, it is only because the number of them is infinitely greater.
author: Homer

text: War is not a courtesy but the most horrible thing in life. Get rid of falsehood and let war be war, not a game.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Stephan Arkadievich chose neither his attitudes nor his opinions, no, the attitudes and opinions came to him on their own, just as he chose neither the style of his hat nor of his coats but got what people were wearing.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Men's consciences cannot be set at rest by new excuses, but only by a change of life which will make any justification of oneself unnecessary as there will be nothing needing justification.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Tell people patriotism is an evil, and most will agree, but with a reservation: 'Wrong patriotism is evil; but there is another kind, the kind we hold.' Yet, what this good patriotism is, no one explains.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Patriotism, or Peace? (1896), translated by Nathan Haskell Dole

text: Six feet of land was all that he needed.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: How Much Land Does a Man Need? (1886)

text: Just as a boa constrictor besmears its victim with foul slime before devouring it, so too do many refuse to contemplate military action unless they first believe their enemy utterly vile. When an army believes the enemy vermin, barbarity easily follows, a condemnation as unjust as it is dangerous.
author: Winston Churchill
source: The River War: An Historical Account of the Reconquest of the Soudan (1899), Volume II pp. 394–395

text: The ability to foretell what is going to happen tomorrow, next week, next month, and next year – and to have the ability afterwards to explain why it didn't happen.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Newspaper interview (1902), when asked what qualities a politician required, Halle, Kay, Irrepressible Churchill. Cleveland: World, 1966. cited in Churchill by Himself (2008), ed. Langworth, PublicAffairs, p. 489 ISBN 1586486381

text: To impose an imaginary state of government on others by violence is not only a vulgar superstition, but a criminal work. Far from assuring humanity's well-being, it is a lie, a more or less unconscious hypocrisy, camouflaging our lowest passions.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Passage written for for The Law of Love and the Law of Violence (1908), released in 1917, as quoted in Equality in Liberty and Justice (2001) by Antony Flew, p. 89

text: I have lived through much, and now I think I have found what is needed for happiness: a quiet, secluded life in the country, being useful to those unaccustomed to kindness; then work one hopes may be of some use; then rest, nature, books, music, love for one's neighbor. Such is my idea of happiness. And then, on top of all that, you for a mate, and children perhaps—what more can the heart of man desire?
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Seven cities warred for Homer, being dead, Who, living, had no roof to shroud his head.
author: Homer
source: Thomas Heywood , The Hierarchy of the Blessed Angels (1635).

text: If people understood they are sons of God, not of fatherlands or Governments, and thus neither slaves nor enemies, then those insane, pernicious organizations called Governments, and all the sufferings they occasion, would cease.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Science may claim to work for science, promising future accessibility. But art, if it be art, should be accessible to all, especially those for whom it is produced. Our art's current state strikingly indicts its producers for their unwillingness or inability to serve the people.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: They denounce all violence save their own, uphold all law save that which they choose to break. They welcome force applied to others, yet demand immunity for themselves, selecting from the Statute-book which laws to obey and which to resist.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Speech in Bradford (14 March 1914), quoted in The Times (16 March 1914), p. 13

text: Service through science and art flourishes only when men live among the people, offering their gifts without claim, for the people to freely accept or decline.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: He was a cut flower in a vase; fair to see, yet bound to die, and to die very soon if the water was not constantly renewed.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Concerning Admiral von Spee’s East Asia Squadron

text: It was Homer who gave laws to the artist; it was Homer who inspired the poet.
author: Homer
source: Francis Wayland , The Iliad and the Bible , reported in Hoyt's New Cyclopedia Of Practical Quotations (1922), pp. 45 & 609.

text: The more is given the less the people will work for themselves, and the less they work the more their poverty will increase.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Help for the Starving , Pt. III (January 1892)

text: Respect was invented to cover the empty place where love should be.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: (voice of Anna) C. Garnett, trans. (New York: 2003), Part 7, Chapter 24 p. 685

text: Are vitality and creativity somehow connected with bellicosity? Could there have been Greek civilisation without this restless obsession with fighting?
author: Homer

text: All state obligations are against the conscience of a Christian: the oath of allegiance, taxes, law proceedings and military service.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter VII , Significance of Compulsory Service
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: In the Odyssey one may liken Homer to the setting sun, of which the grandeur remains without the intensity.
author: Homer

text: Of all books extant in all kinds, Homer is the first and best.
author: Homer
source: George Chapman , The Iliads of Homer (1611), Preface to the Reader.

text: It is easier to steal a club from Hercules than a verse from Homer.
author: Homer
source: It is easier to steal the club of Hercules than a line from Homer.

text: The perversion of Christ's teaching by the Church is most apparent in the absence of any commandment of non-resistance to evil by force.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: Keep your friends close, and your enemies closer.
author: Sun Tzu
source: This has often been attributed to Sun Tzu and sometimes to Petrarch . It comes most directly from a line spoken by Michael Corleone in The Godfather Part II (1974), written by Mario Puzo and Francis Ford Coppola : My father taught me many things here. He taught me in this room. He taught me: keep your friends close but your enemies closer.

text: I do not repudiate science, the reasonable activity of humanity, nor art, its expression. Rather, I speak on their behalf, that mankind may escape the savage state into which it rapidly lapses through false teaching.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Slavery exists in full vigor, though we do not perceive it. Just as serfdom was once deemed a natural condition, so too do people today consider the laborer's plight natural. Yet, as with serfdom, this condition is wrong, unjust, and immoral, and demands alteration.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Victory is reserved for those who are willing to pay its price.
author: Sun Tzu
source: Attributed to Sun Tzu in multiple books and internet sites, but this text does not appear in The Art of War and seems to be a more recent creation.

text: Modern history is like a deaf person, answering questions no one has asked. The fundamental question, 'What power moves peoples?', remains unanswered, rendering all other replies incomprehensible.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Vol 2, pt 5, p 236 — Selected Works, Moscow, 1869

text: No matter what books may sanction it, it is more clearly written in the human heart that animals are to be pitied and not slain, no more than human beings. We know this, if we do not choke the voice of our conscience.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: The Pathway of Life: Teaching Love and Wisdom (posthumous), Part I, International Book Publishing Company, New York, 1919, p. 68

text: Armies are necessary, before all things, for the defense of governments from their own oppressed and enslaved subjects.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter VII , Significance of Compulsory Service
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: Condemn me if you choose, for I do that myself; but condemn me, not the path I follow and reveal to others.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: "Letter to N.N.," quoted by Havelock Ellis in "The New Spirit" (1892) p. 226

text: Nothing lives like his fame and works on the lips of men. Homer provides a noble farce in which, over centuries, Kings, Republics, and Emperors all play their parts, with this great universe as the theatre.
author: Homer
source: Michel de Montaigne , 'On the most excellent of men', The Complete Essays , translated by M. A. Screech (1991; 2003), pp. 852-853

text: I wanted a life of challenge, not what I had; I wanted feeling to guide us in life, not life to guide us in feeling.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: A refined lady devours animal carcasses, convinced of her rightness, while asserting two contradictions: she is too delicate for vegetable food, yet too sensitive to inflict suffering. Her weakness stems from unnatural food, and she cannot avoid causing suffering, for she eats them.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: An army is not a field for Utopian political theories, but a weapon for the defense of the State.
author: Winston Churchill
source: British Cavalry , The Anglo-Saxon Review, March 1901.

text: I searched for an answer in every area of human knowledge, not casually, but painfully, persistently, day and night, like a dying man seeking salvation. I found nothing.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: The Times is speechless, and takes three columns to express its speechlessness.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Speech at Kinnaird Hall, Dundee, Scotland ("The Dundee Election"), May 14, 1908, in Liberalism and the Social Problem (1909), Churchill, BiblioBazaar (Second Edition, 2006), p. 148 ISBN 1426451989

text: Sure I am of this, that you have only to endure to conquer. You have only to persevere to save yourselves, and to save all those who rely upon you. You have only to go right on, and at the end of the road, be it short or long, victory and honor will be found.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Remarks at the Guildhall, 4 September 1914, after the first British naval victory of World War I, the sinking of three German cruisers in the Battle of Heligoland Bight , as cited in Churchill: A Life , Martin Gilbert, Macmillan (1992), p. 279 : ISBN 0805023968

text: The hatred and contempt of the oppressed people are increasing, and the physical and moral strength of the richer classes are decreasing: the deceit which supports all this is wearing out, and the rich classes have nothing wherewith to comfort themselves.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: What is to be Done (1899) p. 262

text: There is no greatness where there is not simplicity, goodness, and truth.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Anyone can rat, but it takes a certain amount of ingenuity to re-rat.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Remark in 1923 after rejoining the Conservatives, having left them earlier to join the Liberals; reported in Kay Halle, Irrepressible Churchill (1966), p. 52–53. Other sources say this remark was made in 1924.

text: It occurred to him that his scarcely perceptible attempts to struggle against what was considered good, those impulses he had suppressed, might have been the real thing, and all the rest false. He tried to defend his life, and suddenly felt the weakness of what he was defending.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: To improve is to change, so to be perfect is to have changed often.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Winston Churchill (June 23, 1925), His complete speeches, 1897–1963 , edited by Robert Rhodes James, Chelsea House ed., vol. 4 (1922–1928), p. 3706. During a debate with Philip Snowden, 1st Viscount Snowden.

text: The happiness of men consists in life. And life is in labor.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: What Is To Be Done? (1886) Chap. XXXVIII, as translated in The Novels and Other Works of Lyof N. Tolstoï (1902) edited by Nathan Haskell Dole, p. 259

text: The attitude of the ruling classes to the laborers is that of a man who has felled his adversary to the earth and holds him down, not so much because he wants to hold him down, as because he knows that if he let him go, even for a second, he would himself be stabbed, for his adversary is infuriated and has a knife in his hand. Their whole life and all their enjoyments are embittered either by the stings of conscience or by terror.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter V , Contradiction Between our Life and our Christian Conscience
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: History is the life of nations and of humanity. To seize and put into words, to describe directly the life of humanity or even of a single nation, appears impossible.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Governments create nothing and have nothing to give but what they have first taken away.
author: Winston Churchill
source: "Why I am a Free Trader," Chapter I in T.W. Stead's journal Coming Men on Coming Questions (April 13, 1905), bottom p. 9.

text: Man is created for happiness, which lies within him, in the satisfaction of simple human needs; all unhappiness arises not from privation but from superfluity. Nothing in this world is terrible. As there is no condition in which man can be entirely happy or free, so there is none in which he must be unhappy or lack freedom. Suffering and freedom have limits, and those limits are very near together.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: We acknowledge him the father of poetical diction, the first who taught that language of the gods to men.
author: Homer

text: In war, numbers alone confer no advantage. Do not advance relying on sheer military power.
author: Sun Tzu
source_section: Chapter IX · Movement and Development of Troops

text: He knew she was there by the joy and fear that overwhelmed his heart.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Build your opponent a golden bridge to retreat across.
author: Sun Tzu
source: This has appeared as a variant of Sun Tzu's assertion to "leave a way of escape." Tu Mu, commenting on Sun Tzu, advises, "Show him there is a road to safety..." Ch. 7; it has also recently appeared on the internet attributed to Scipio Africanus , but without citation.

text: The vocation of every man and woman is to serve other people.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: What Is To Be Done? (1886) Chap. XL, as translated in The Novels and Other Works of Lyof N. Tolstoï (1902) edited by Nathan Haskell Dole, p. 281

text: Homer made the childhood of poetry and other arts adult, complete, and mature. He can be called 'the first poet and the last': for before him, none could he imitate; after him, none could imitate him.
author: Homer
source: Michel de Montaigne , 'On the most excellent of men', The Complete Essays , translated by M. A. Screech (1991; 2003), pp. 851-852

text: What is the power and glory of Caesar and Alexander compared to the genius of a ballad-maker who has been dead two thousand seven hundred years?
author: Homer
source: Thomas Babington Macaulay to his niece Margaret (August 1851), quoted in George Otto Trevelyan, The Life and Letters of Lord Macaulay, Volume II (1876), pp. 186-187

text: Between the Churches as institutions and Christianity, not only is there nothing in common but the name, but they are two quite opposite and opposing principles. The one represents pride, violence, self-assertion, immobility, and death; the other, humility, penitence, meekness, progress, and life.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter III , Christianity Misunderstood by Believers
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: Reason has discovered the struggle for existence and the law that I must throttle all those who hinder the satisfaction of my desires. That is the deduction reason makes. But the law of loving others could not be discovered by reason, because it is unreasonable.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Though the doctors treated him, let his blood, and gave him medications to drink, he nevertheless recovered.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: For a nation to try to tax itself into prosperity is like a man standing in a bucket and trying to lift himself up by the handle.
author: Winston Churchill
source: From "Why I am a Free Trader" (1905), Churchill revised this several times, the earliest recorded version coming from the speech "For Free Trade" at the Free Trade Hall, Manchester, 19 Feb­ru­ary 1904: It is the the­ory of the Pro­tec­tion­ist that imports are an evil. He thinks that if you shut out the for­eign imported man­u­fac­tured goods you will make these goods your­selves, in addi­tion to the goods which you make now, includ­ing those goods which we make to exchange for the for­eign goods that come in. If a man can believe that he can believe any­thing. (Laugh­ter.) We Free-traders say it is not true. To think you can make a man richer by putting on a tax is like a man think­ing that he can stand in a bucket and lift him­self up by the han­dle. (Laugh­ter and cheers.) [ 2 ] :Vol.I: 261

text: Our pleasures—our dinners, plays, and festivities—are enjoyed only by grace of the policeman's revolver or the soldier's rifle, poised to shoot down the famished outcast who, robbed of his share, watches our revelry with covetous eyes.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Chapter XII , Conclusion—Repent Ye, for the Kingdom of Heaven is at Hand
source_section: The Kingdom of God is Within You (1894)

text: Our false way of life, propped up by sciences and arts, grows only feebler, sicklier, and more tormenting each year, deepening the gloom of our lives. Salvation is not found in comforts or pleasures, but only in a fundamental change of life.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Strategy without tactics is the slowest route to victory. Tactics without strategy is the noise before defeat.
author: Sun Tzu
source: Probably apocryphal. This quotation does not appear in any print translation of Sun Tzu. The first citation in Google Books is from 2002; no citation in Google Books occurs in a translation of Sun Tzu.

text: I wonder whether any other generation has seen such astounding revolutions of data and values as those through which we have lived. Scarcely anything material or established which I was brought up to believe was permanent and vital, has lasted. Everything I was sure or taught to be sure was impossible, has happened.
author: Winston Churchill
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: Economic and constitutional questions, the fiscal system and form of government, are but means to an end: to create conditions favorable to the social and moral welfare of the citizens.
author: Winston Churchill

text: We are with Europe, but not of it. We are linked, but not comprised. We are interested and associated, but not absorbed. Should European statesmen ask, 'Wouldest thou be spoken for to the king, or captain of the host?', we reply, 'I dwell among mine own people.'
author: Winston Churchill
source: "The United States of Europe", The Saturday Evening Post (15 February 1930)

text: Homer's poems were born of a free fury, an absolute and full soul; Virgil's, of a courtly, laborious, and altogether imitatory spirit. Homer's books have been precedents for all poems, imitating none, nor ever worthily imitated by any.
author: Homer
source: George Chapman , dedication to Achilles' Shield (1598).

text: We have engrossed to ourselves a disproportionate share of the world's wealth and traffic. Our claim to unmolested enjoyment of vast possessions, mainly acquired by violence and maintained by force, often seems less reasonable to others than to us.
author: Winston Churchill
source: In a comment to his British Cabinet colleagues in January 1914 in a confidential paper. Cited in John Darwin, The Empire Project , Cambridge 2010, p268.

text: Poetry changes with every generation, but it does not improve or progress. It just changes its styles, trappings and some of its obsessions, but we can still enjoy Sappho and Homer; they are today's news as much as when they were written or recited.
author: Homer
source: Marge Piercy "WHY SPECULATE ON THE FUTURE?" in My Life, My Body (2015)

text: There was in her the glow of the real diamond among glass imitations, shining out in her exquisite, truly enigmatic eyes. Everyone looking into those eyes fancied he knew her wholly, and knowing her, could not but love her.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: Anna's thoughts about Liza, Part III, Chapter 13

text: Anarchists are right in their negation of the existing order, and in asserting that, without authority, there could be no worse violence than that of authority itself. They are mistaken only in thinking Anarchy can be instituted by revolution; it will arise only when more people no longer require protection from governmental power, and are ashamed of applying it.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: "On Anarchy", in Pamphlets : Translated from the Russian (1900) as translated by Aylmer Maude, p. 22

text: Governments, by replacing the eternal moral law within men's hearts with their own unjust, expedient laws, do not deter crime but rather increase it and lower society's moral standard.
author: Leo Tolstoy
source: The Meaning of the Russian Revolution (1906), a work about the 1905 Russian Revolution .

text: Everything comes in time to him who knows how to wait.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: Greek tragedy could never have flowered without Homer as its pioneer.
author: Homer

text: If the slave-owner of our times has no slave, John, whom he can send to the cesspool, he has five shillings, of which hundreds of such Johns are in such need that the slave-owner of our times may choose any one out of hundreds of Johns and be a benefactor to him by giving him the preference, and allowing him, rather than another, to climb down into the cesspool.
author: Leo Tolstoy

text: What is the use of living, if not to strive for noble causes and make this muddled world a better place for those who follow? How else can we align with the infinite and eternal? I avow my faith that we march towards better days; humanity will not be cast down, for behind the distant mountains is the promise of the sun.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Speech at Kinnaird Hall, Dundee, Scotland ("Unemployment"), October 10, 1908, in Liberalism and the Social Problem (1909), Churchill, Echo Library (2007), p. 87 ISBN 1406845817

text: The one thing missing from The Art of War is love, any sense of altruism, or loving your neighbor; it is about how to take advantage of, triumph over, and manipulate your neighbor. The Analects of Confucius, however, is a superior book because it talks about morality and caring for your fellow human being. The Art of War is the dark side of Chinese culture, a powerfully polluting book we must be aware of but not corrupted by.
author: Sun Tzu
source: John Minford , Lecture on Culture and Translation at Hang Seng Management College (20 February 2016)

text: They say no woman could possibly have written the Odyssey . To me, on the other hand, it seems even less possible that a man could have done so. As for its being by a practised and elderly writer, nothing but youth and inexperience could produce anything so naïve and so lovely.
author: Homer
